topics.title: Why Deep Learning? From Features to Representations
topics.summary: This session motivates deep learning by contrasting hand-crafted feature engineering (SIFT, HOG, Bag-of-Words) with automatic representation learning, explaining why classical ML plateaus on large unstructured data while deep networks keep improving with scale. It introduces the Universal Approximation Theorem and its limits, the hierarchical features that emerge from end-to-end training, and a practical data-size vs. data-type framework for choosing between classical ML and deep learning, anchored by the 2012 AlexNet/ImageNet breakthrough.

notes.heading: Hand-Crafted Features Don't Scale
notes.body:
- **Feature engineering** = humans manually design the inputs a model sees: **SIFT/HOG** (image gradients), **Bag-of-Words / TF-IDF** (text), MFCCs (audio).
- Works well for **tabular/structured data** where columns already carry meaning.
- **Breaks down** for images, audio, raw text: high-dimensional, the relevant features are unknown, and pixel-level signals are entangled.
- **The features are the ceiling**: a fixed feature extractor caps accuracy. Once features are 'maxed out', more data cannot help — the model can only fit a better boundary in an already-lossy feature space.
- Contrast: deep nets fold feature extraction *into* learning, so their effective feature space grows richer with more data.

notes.heading: The Scaling / 'Andrew Ng' Plot
notes.body:
- Plot **test accuracy (y)** vs **training-set size (x)** for classical ML (fixed features) vs deep nets.
- **Classical ML with fixed features**: rises, then **plateaus** — extra data stops helping because representations are frozen.
- **Deep learning**: starts lower (data-hungry, can overfit when small), but **keeps climbing** and eventually **overtakes** classical ML at large data sizes.
- Key crossover insight: at small $N$ classical ML often wins; at large $N$ deep learning dominates.
- This is *why* the field shifted: data + compute made the right half of the plot reachable.

notes.heading: Universal Approximation Theorem (UAT)
notes.body:
- **Statement (informal):** a feed-forward network with a **single hidden layer** and a **non-polynomial** activation (e.g. sigmoid, ReLU) can approximate **any continuous function** on a compact set to arbitrary accuracy, given **enough hidden units**.
$$\forall\,\varepsilon>0,\ \exists\, N,\ \sup_{x\in K}\Big| f(x) - \sum_{i=1}^{N} v_i\,\sigma(w_i^\top x + b_i) \Big| < \varepsilon$$
- **What it does NOT tell you:**
  - *How many* neurons $N$ are needed (could be astronomically large).
  - *How to find* the weights (existence $\ne$ trainability; gradient descent may not reach them).
  - *How much data* is needed to generalize from finite samples.
- It is an **existence** result, not a guarantee of efficiency, optimization success, or superiority over SVMs/other models.
- **Depth vs width:** UAT uses width; in practice **depth** approximates many functions far more **parameter-efficiently** (exponential separation results), motivating *deep* nets.

notes.heading: Representation Learning (the paradigm shift)
notes.body:
- Instead of hand-designing features, the network **learns hierarchical representations** end-to-end:
$$\textbf{edges} \rightarrow \textbf{textures} \rightarrow \textbf{parts} \rightarrow \textbf{objects}$$
- Early layers detect generic low-level patterns (oriented edges, color blobs); deeper layers compose them into class-specific, abstract concepts.
- **Nobody programs this** — it *emerges* from minimizing the loss over data (end-to-end training).
- **Transferability:** features learned on ImageNet transfer to new domains (e.g. medical imaging) — the basis of **transfer learning / fine-tuning**.
- Evidence (Zeiler & Fergus): visualizing feature maps shows increasing abstraction with depth; class separation (e.g. via t-SNE of activations) improves at deeper layers.

notes.heading: When to Use DL vs Classical ML (2x2 framework)
notes.body:
- Decision axes: **data size** and **data type (structured vs unstructured)**.

| | Small data | Large data |
|---|---|---|
| **Tabular/structured** | Classical ML (trees, SVM, linear) | Gradient-boosted trees / DL both viable |
| **Unstructured (image/text/audio)** | Transfer learning / classical features | **Deep learning** |

- **Choose classical ML** when: small data, strong tabular features, **interpretability** required, **limited compute**, low latency.
- **Choose deep learning** when: large **unstructured** datasets, features are unknown/hard to design, transfer learning available.
- **Small unstructured data (e.g. 500 X-rays):** do NOT train a big CNN from scratch (overfits) — use **transfer learning / fine-tuning** a pretrained CNN, or classical features.

notes.heading: AlexNet & The 2012 ImageNet Moment
notes.body:
- **AlexNet (Krizhevsky, Sutskever, Hinton, 2012)** won the **ImageNet (ILSVRC)** challenge by a huge margin, igniting the deep learning era.
- **Surprising because no single idea was new** — the win came from the **combination**:
  - **Deep CNN** architecture
  - **ReLU** activations (faster training, mitigates vanishing gradients)
  - **GPU training** (made deep nets computationally feasible)
  - **Dropout** (regularization to fight overfitting)
  - **Large labeled data** (ImageNet, ~1.2M training images) + data augmentation
- Lesson: **integration + scale (data + compute)** unlocked a capability latent in older ideas.

notes.heading: Roadmap of the DL Landscape
notes.body:
- This session previews the model families covered over the course:
  - **CNNs** — images / spatial data (convolution shares weights, exploits locality and translation equivariance).
  - **RNNs / LSTMs** — sequences (text, time series).
  - **Transformers** — attention-based sequence/vision models; core op $\text{softmax}\!\left(\tfrac{QK^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V$.
  - **Generative models** — GANs, VAEs, diffusion.
- Unifying theme: **learn representations from raw data** rather than hand-engineer them.
- Foundational tools previewed for later weeks: gradient descent, backprop chain rule $\frac{\partial L}{\partial w} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat y}\frac{\partial \hat y}{\partial z}\frac{\partial z}{\partial w}$, softmax + cross-entropy classification.

notes.heading: Key Formulas to Remember
notes.body:
- **Single neuron / logistic output:** $$\hat y = \sigma(w^\top x + b),\quad \sigma(z)=\frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$$
- **Softmax (multiclass):** $$p_i = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}$$
- **Cross-entropy loss (one-hot $y$):** $$L = -\sum_i y_i \log p_i$$
- **UAT approximation:** $\hat f(x)=\sum_{i=1}^{N} v_i\,\sigma(w_i^\top x+b_i)$, with sup-norm error $<\varepsilon$ for enough units $N$.
- **(Preview) ReLU:** $\text{ReLU}(z)=\max(0,z)$ — the activation AlexNet popularized.

quiz.q: Why does adding more training data eventually stop helping a classical ML model that uses a *fixed* hand-crafted feature extractor?
options: Because more data always causes overfitting in classical models
Because the fixed features form a ceiling — they cannot represent more information regardless of data quantity
Because classical models cannot use more than a few thousand samples by definition
Because gradient descent diverges with large datasets
quiz.explanation: With frozen features, the representation is the bottleneck: once the feature space is 'maxed out', extra data only marginally refines the decision boundary and accuracy plateaus. Deep nets instead improve their *features* with data. Overfitting is not the inherent cause, and classical models are not capped at a few thousand samples.

quiz.q: A colleague claims: 'The Universal Approximation Theorem proves neural networks are always better than SVMs.' What is the core flaw?
options: The UAT only applies to SVMs, not neural networks
The UAT guarantees a solution *exists* but says nothing about trainability, parameter count, data needs, or actual performance vs other models
The UAT requires infinitely deep networks, which is impossible
The UAT only holds for discrete functions, not continuous ones
quiz.explanation: UAT is an existence result: enough neurons can approximate any continuous function on a compact set. It does not say how many neurons, whether gradient descent will find them, how much data is needed, or that the net beats an SVM in practice. It applies to (shallow) neural nets and covers continuous functions.

quiz.q: You have 500 labeled chest X-rays. What is the most sensible approach?
options: Train a large CNN from scratch to maximize capacity
Use transfer learning (fine-tune a pretrained CNN) or classical features, since 500 images is too few to train a big net from scratch
Use the Universal Approximation Theorem to compute the exact network size
Discard deep learning entirely because medical images are unstructured
quiz.explanation: 500 images is very small; a large CNN trained from scratch would overfit badly. Transfer learning leverages features learned on large datasets (which transfer across domains), or one can use classical features. UAT gives no recipe for sizing, and unstructured data does not forbid DL.

quiz.q: AlexNet's 2012 ImageNet victory was surprising mainly because:
options: It invented the convolution operation for the first time
It used a brand-new optimizer never seen before
None of its individual components were new — the breakthrough came from combining deep CNNs, ReLU, GPU training, dropout, and large data
It required no training data at all
quiz.explanation: Convolutions, ReLU, dropout, and GPUs pre-existed. The win came from *integrating* them at scale on ImageNet. So the surprise was that a combination of known ideas plus data/compute produced a leap, not a single novel invention.

quiz.q: In the 'representation learning' view, what does a deep image network typically learn from shallow to deep layers?
options: Objects first, then parts, then edges (deep to shallow abstraction)
Edges → textures → parts → objects (increasing abstraction with depth)
Random features that have no interpretable structure
Only color histograms at every layer
quiz.explanation: Empirically (and per the Zeiler & Fergus visualizations), early layers learn generic low-level patterns like edges; deeper layers compose them into textures, parts, and eventually whole objects. Abstraction increases with depth, not the reverse.

quiz.q: Which statement best captures *why depth* matters, beyond what the UAT (which uses a single wide hidden layer) promises?
options: Depth is irrelevant; a single hidden layer is always equally efficient
Depth can represent certain functions with exponentially fewer parameters than an equivalent shallow network
Deeper networks always train faster than shallow ones with no downsides
Depth removes the need for any training data
quiz.explanation: UAT shows a single hidden layer suffices in principle, but it may need impractically many units. Depth gives parameter-efficiency: certain functions need exponentially fewer parameters when expressed by deep compositions. Depth does not eliminate data needs or guarantee faster training (it can even worsen optimization without care).

quiz.q: On the 'Andrew Ng' scaling plot (test accuracy vs training-set size), which behavior is expected?
options: Classical-ML curve keeps rising indefinitely while DL plateaus early
Both curves are flat and identical
Classical ML plateaus while deep learning keeps improving and eventually overtakes it at large data
Deep learning always wins at every training-set size, including the smallest
quiz.explanation: Fixed-feature classical ML saturates; deep learning, though data-hungry and weaker at small N, continues to improve and surpasses classical ML at large N. DL does not necessarily win at small data, where it can overfit.

quiz.q: Which scenario most favors *classical ML* over deep learning?
options: A 10-million-image labeling task
Small tabular dataset where interpretability and limited compute matter
Speech recognition from raw audio at scale
Machine translation over millions of sentence pairs
quiz.explanation: Classical ML shines on small, structured/tabular data, when interpretability is required, and under compute constraints. The other options are large unstructured-data problems where deep learning dominates.

quiz.q: For a $K$-class single-label neural classifier, which output+loss pairing is standard?
options: Sigmoid on each logit independently with mean-squared error
Softmax over logits with cross-entropy loss
ReLU on logits with hinge loss
No activation on logits with L1 loss
quiz.explanation: Softmax turns logits into a probability distribution $p_i=e^{z_i}/\sum_j e^{z_j}$, and cross-entropy $L=-\sum_i y_i\log p_i$ is its natural matched loss. The other pairings are non-standard for single-label multiclass classification.

quiz.q: Why do ImageNet-pretrained features often work for medical imaging, a very different domain?
options: Because medical and natural images have identical pixel statistics
Because low/mid-level learned features (edges, textures) are generic and transferable, so they provide a strong starting point
Because the UAT guarantees transfer to any domain
Because pretrained models ignore the input and memorize labels
quiz.explanation: Early-layer features (edges, textures, simple shapes) are broadly useful across visual domains, enabling transfer learning even when the target domain differs. UAT says nothing about transfer, and pretrained nets do use the input.

quiz.q: What is the role of **ReLU** in making deep networks like AlexNet practical?
options: It guarantees the network is convex and has a unique global minimum
It is a fast, non-saturating activation that speeds training and helps mitigate vanishing gradients vs sigmoid/tanh
It removes the need for any nonlinearity
It performs the convolution operation
quiz.explanation: $\text{ReLU}(z)=\max(0,z)$ does not saturate for positive inputs, so gradients flow better than with sigmoid/tanh, accelerating training of deep nets. It does not make the loss convex, and it *is* the nonlinearity (it does not convolve).

quiz.q: Which of the following is a correct limitation of the Universal Approximation Theorem?
options: It proves gradient descent will always find the optimal weights
It specifies the exact minimal number of neurons for any target function
It is silent on how many neurons are required, how to train them, and how much data is needed
It only guarantees approximation of linear functions
quiz.explanation: UAT guarantees existence of an approximator for continuous functions but gives no construction: not the neuron count, not a training procedure, not data requirements. It is not restricted to linear functions and makes no optimization guarantees.

descriptive.q: Explain why more training data continues to help deep learning but eventually stops helping classical ML built on fixed hand-crafted features. Tie your answer to the 'features are the ceiling' idea.
descriptive.answer:
With **classical ML on fixed features**, the feature extractor (e.g. HOG, SIFT, Bag-of-Words) is designed once and frozen. The model can only learn a decision rule *within* that pre-defined feature space. Once enough data has been seen to estimate that boundary well, additional data cannot inject information the features fail to capture — the **representation is the ceiling**, so accuracy **plateaus**.

**Deep learning** is different because the features themselves are *learned parameters*, optimized jointly with the classifier (end-to-end). More data lets the network discover **richer, more discriminative representations** rather than just refining a boundary in a fixed space. As long as model capacity and optimization keep up, performance keeps climbing.

This is exactly the **scaling ('Andrew Ng') plot**: the classical curve saturates while the DL curve keeps rising and eventually overtakes it at large $N$. The trade-off: at *small* $N$, DL can overfit and may underperform classical ML, so the advantage appears only with sufficient data.

descriptive.q: State the Universal Approximation Theorem precisely and explain three things it does NOT guarantee. Why does this make 'UAT proves neural nets beat SVMs' a fallacy?
descriptive.answer:
**Statement:** A feed-forward network with a single hidden layer and a non-polynomial activation $\sigma$ can approximate any continuous function $f$ on a compact domain $K$ to arbitrary accuracy: for any $\varepsilon>0$ there exist $N$ units and weights with
$$\sup_{x\in K}\Big| f(x) - \sum_{i=1}^{N} v_i\,\sigma(w_i^\top x + b_i)\Big| < \varepsilon.$$

**What it does NOT guarantee:**
1. **How many neurons** $N$ are needed — possibly astronomically large.
2. **Trainability** — existence of good weights does not mean gradient descent will *find* them; the loss landscape may be hard.
3. **Data efficiency / generalization** — it says nothing about how much data is needed to learn the approximation from finite samples.

**Why the SVM claim is wrong:** UAT is an *existence* theorem about representational capacity, not about *learnability* or *comparative performance*. It does not promise fewer parameters, faster training, less data, or better test accuracy than an SVM. In practice an SVM can outperform a neural net (e.g. on small tabular data). So 'a solution exists' $\ne$ 'neural nets are always better.'

descriptive.q: Compare hand-crafted feature engineering with representation learning. Give the hierarchy deep nets learn and explain why it is powerful and transferable.
descriptive.answer:
**Hand-crafted features:** a human decides what is informative (gradients for HOG, keypoints for SIFT, word counts for BOW). This is interpretable and effective on **structured/tabular** data, but it requires domain expertise, is brittle on complex raw signals (images/audio/text), and imposes a fixed ceiling on accuracy.

**Representation learning:** the network learns features automatically by minimizing the loss end-to-end. For images the learned hierarchy is roughly:
$$\textbf{edges} \rightarrow \textbf{textures} \rightarrow \textbf{parts} \rightarrow \textbf{objects}.$$
Early layers capture generic low-level patterns; deeper layers compose them into abstract, class-specific concepts. **Nobody programs this** — it emerges from training.

**Why powerful & transferable:** because low/mid-level features (edges, textures) are *generic*, the same early-layer representations are useful across many tasks and even across domains (ImageNet features transfer to medical imaging). This underpins **transfer learning / fine-tuning**, which is especially valuable when the target dataset is small.

descriptive.q: You must build a classifier from only 500 labeled chest X-rays. Walk through your decision and justify it using the data-size/data-type framework.
descriptive.answer:
**Data type:** images = **unstructured**, which normally favors deep learning. **Data size:** 500 is *very small*, which strongly disfavors training a large model from scratch.

**Decision:** Do **not** train a big CNN from scratch — it would **overfit** badly (high capacity, tiny data). Instead:
- **Transfer learning / fine-tuning:** start from a CNN pretrained on a large dataset (e.g. ImageNet), freeze or lightly fine-tune early layers (generic edge/texture features transfer), and retrain the final layers on the X-rays. This injects strong priors and dramatically reduces data needs.
- **Or classical ML on good features** (possibly features extracted from a pretrained net) with strong regularization, if interpretability/compute is a constraint.
- Add **data augmentation** and **cross-validation** to squeeze more signal from 500 images.

**Justification:** The 2×2 framework says unstructured data leans DL, but small data leans classical/transfer. Transfer learning is the sweet spot: it keeps DL's representational power while avoiding the overfitting that pure from-scratch training would cause at $N=500$.

descriptive.q: Why was AlexNet's 2012 ImageNet win surprising even though none of its individual components were novel? What general lesson about progress in ML does this illustrate?
descriptive.answer:
Each ingredient existed before 2012: **convolutional networks** (LeCun, late 1980s/90s), **ReLU**, **dropout**-style regularization ideas, and **GPU** computation. AlexNet's contribution was the **combination at scale**:
- a **deep CNN** architecture,
- **ReLU** activations (fast, non-saturating training),
- **GPU** training (made the computation feasible),
- **dropout** (regularization against overfitting),
- a **large labeled dataset** (ImageNet, ~1.2M training images) plus augmentation.

The surprise was that integrating known ideas — together with enough **data and compute** — produced a *step-change* in accuracy, beating hand-engineered pipelines by a wide margin.

**General lesson:** Breakthroughs often come not from a single new algorithm but from **scaling and integrating** existing ideas once data and hardware cross a threshold. Capability can be latent in old methods, waiting for the right resources to unlock it.

descriptive.q: Sketch and explain the 'Andrew Ng' scaling plot. What axes does it use, what are the two curves, and how would you reason about the crossover point?
descriptive.answer:
**Axes:** x = **training-set size** $N$ (often log scale); y = **test accuracy** (or performance).

**Two curves:**
- **Classical ML (fixed features):** rises quickly at small $N$, then **plateaus** — frozen features cap accuracy.
- **Deep learning:** lower at small $N$ (data-hungry, can overfit) but **keeps climbing** and eventually surpasses classical ML.

**Crossover reasoning:** At small $N$ the classical curve is typically *above* the DL curve (DL hasn't enough data to learn good representations and may overfit). As $N$ grows, DL's learned features improve while classical ML stalls, so the curves **cross**; beyond that point DL is preferred. The exact crossover depends on task difficulty, data type, model capacity, and regularization. Practically: estimate your available $N$ and data type, and pick the method that is on the winning side of the crossover for your regime.

descriptive.q: Contrast the conditions under which you would choose deep learning vs classical ML. Include interpretability, compute, and data considerations.
descriptive.answer:
**Choose deep learning when:**
- Data is **large** and **unstructured** (images, audio, text).
- Good features are **unknown or hard to engineer** — let the net learn them.
- **Transfer learning** is available to offset limited target data.
- Sufficient **compute (GPUs)** and tolerance for less interpretability.

**Choose classical ML when:**
- Data is **small** and/or **tabular** with informative columns.
- **Interpretability** is required (e.g. regulated domains) — linear models / trees are easier to explain.
- **Compute is limited** or low-latency/edge deployment is needed.
- Strong hand-crafted features already exist.

**Summary (2×2):** axes are *data size* × *data type*. Large + unstructured → DL; small + tabular → classical ML; small + unstructured → transfer learning; large + tabular → either (often gradient-boosted trees are a strong baseline). The choice balances representational power against data, compute, and interpretability constraints.

descriptive.q: Although the UAT only needs one wide hidden layer, modern networks are *deep*. Explain the practical motivation for depth and what 'end-to-end training' means.
descriptive.answer:
**Why depth despite UAT:** UAT guarantees a single hidden layer *can* approximate any continuous function, but possibly with an **impractically large width**. **Depth** provides **parameter efficiency**: composing simple nonlinear transformations lets the network represent certain complex/compositional functions with **exponentially fewer parameters** than a shallow net would need. Depth also induces a natural **feature hierarchy** (edges → textures → parts → objects), reusing lower-level features to build higher-level ones — which matches the compositional structure of real data and aids generalization and transfer.

**End-to-end training:** rather than separately engineering features and then training a classifier, *all* parameters — from the first feature-extracting layer to the final classifier — are optimized **jointly** by minimizing a single loss (e.g. cross-entropy) via backpropagation. The chain rule propagates gradients through every layer:
$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial w^{(l)}} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat y}\cdots\frac{\partial z^{(l)}}{\partial w^{(l)}}.$$
This lets the network *learn the features that are best for the task*, which is the essence of representation learning and a key reason deep models outperform fixed-feature pipelines on complex data.

topics.title: Perceptron to MLP
topics.summary: This session builds neural networks from a single neuron (logistic regression with a new name) up to multi-layer perceptrons (MLPs). It covers how a neuron computes a weighted sum plus bias followed by an activation, why non-linear activations (sigmoid, tanh, ReLU) are essential, the forward pass through stacked layers, and the XOR problem that proves why depth/hidden layers matter and which contributed to an "AI winter" after Minsky & Papert (1969).

notes.heading: The Single Neuron = Logistic Regression
notes.body:
A neuron computes a **weighted sum** of inputs, adds a **bias**, then applies an **activation function** $\sigma$:
$$z = w^\top x + b = \sum_{i} w_i x_i + b, \qquad \hat y = \sigma(z)$$
- With $\sigma$ = **sigmoid**, this is exactly **logistic regression** — same math, new name.
- $w$ controls the **orientation** of the decision boundary; $b$ shifts it (translates the boundary away from the origin).
- A single neuron can only draw **one straight (linear) decision boundary** — the hyperplane $w^\top x + b = 0$.
- It is **simple but limited**: it can only solve problems that are **linearly separable**.

notes.heading: Why Activation Functions Matter (Non-linearity)
notes.body:
**Without activations, depth is useless.** A stack of linear (affine) layers collapses into a single linear layer because composing linear maps yields a linear map:
$$W_L(\cdots(W_2(W_1 x)))= (W_L\cdots W_1)\,x = W_{\text{eff}}\,x$$
- $\text{linear}\circ\text{linear} = \text{linear}$, so 100 linear layers $\equiv$ 1 linear layer; the bias terms likewise collapse to one effective bias.
- **Activation functions inject non-linearity**, breaking this collapse and letting the network bend decision boundaries.
- Non-linearity is what gives MLPs their power (cf. the **Universal Approximation Theorem**: a single hidden layer with a non-linear activation can approximate any continuous function on a compact set, given enough neurons).

notes.heading: Activation Function Zoo
notes.body:
**Sigmoid:** $\;\sigma(z)=\dfrac{1}{1+e^{-z}}\in(0,1)$, derivative $\sigma'(z)=\sigma(z)\big(1-\sigma(z)\big)\le 0.25$. Good for the **output of binary classification** (a probability); saturates and causes **vanishing gradients** in hidden layers.

**Tanh:** $\;\tanh(z)=\dfrac{e^{z}-e^{-z}}{e^{z}+e^{-z}}\in(-1,1)$, **zero-centered** (better than sigmoid for hidden units) but still saturates; its derivative is $1-\tanh^2(z)\le 1$.

**ReLU:** $\;\mathrm{ReLU}(z)=\max(0,z)$, derivative is $1$ for $z>0$ and $0$ for $z<0$ (undefined at $z=0$, taken as $0$ in practice). **Default for hidden layers** — cheap, non-saturating for positive inputs, mitigates vanishing gradients. Risk: **dying ReLU** (neurons stuck at 0).

**Leaky ReLU:** $\;\max(\alpha z, z)$ with small $\alpha$ (e.g. $0.01$) — keeps a small gradient for $z<0$ to avoid dead neurons.

notes.heading: Vanishing Gradients
notes.body:
- **Sigmoid/tanh saturate**: for large $|z|$ their derivatives go to $0$, so gradients flowing backward are multiplied by tiny numbers.
- In a deep network, backprop multiplies many such small derivatives together: $\prod_k \sigma'(z_k)$ shrinks **exponentially** with depth $\Rightarrow$ early layers barely update.
- Sigmoid's derivative peaks at only $0.25$, so each sigmoid layer shrinks the backpropagated gradient by **at least** $4\times$.
- **ReLU's** derivative is exactly $1$ for active units, so it **does not shrink** positive-side gradients — the main reason ReLU is the hidden-layer default.
- This is the practical reason **sigmoid/tanh struggle in deep networks**.

notes.heading: MLP Architecture & The Forward Pass
notes.body:
An **MLP** stacks layers of neurons; each hidden layer applies **multiply $\to$ add bias $\to$ activate**, repeated:
$$a^{(0)}=x,\qquad z^{(\ell)}=W^{(\ell)}a^{(\ell-1)}+b^{(\ell)},\qquad a^{(\ell)}=\sigma^{(\ell)}\!\big(z^{(\ell)}\big)$$
- **Depth $\to$ expressivity:** *one layer = lines, two layers = (convex) shapes/regions, three layers = essentially any boundary.*
- A weight matrix $W^{(\ell)}\in\mathbb{R}^{n_\ell\times n_{\ell-1}}$ maps the previous layer's activations to the next layer's pre-activations.
- **Fully connected / dense:** every neuron connects to every neuron in the next layer.
- Output activation depends on the task: **sigmoid** (binary), **softmax** (multi-class), **identity/linear** (regression).

notes.heading: The XOR Problem
notes.body:
XOR truth table: $0\oplus0=0,\;0\oplus1=1,\;1\oplus0=1,\;1\oplus1=0$.
- The four points are **not linearly separable** — no single straight line separates the two classes, so a **single neuron cannot solve XOR**.
- A **2-2-1 MLP** (2 inputs $\to$ 2 hidden neurons $\to$ 1 output) solves it:
  - Hidden neuron 1 learns one line; hidden neuron 2 learns another line (e.g. roughly an **OR** boundary and a **NAND** boundary).
  - The output neuron combines them with an **AND-like** operation, carving out the XOR region.
- Intuition: $\text{XOR} = \text{OR} \;\text{AND}\; \text{NAND}$. The hidden layer **re-represents** inputs so the problem becomes linearly separable in the new feature space — this is *why "deep" learning is deep.*

notes.heading: Historical Context: Minsky & Papert 1969
notes.body:
- Minsky & Papert's book *Perceptrons* (1969) analyzed the limits of single-layer perceptrons, including their inability to compute XOR (and other non-linearly-separable functions).
- This was **influential** because the perceptron was the field's fundamental building block — many researchers concluded neural nets were a **dead end**, contributing to an **"AI winter"** of reduced funding and interest.
- The fix (**hidden layers** trained by **backpropagation**) was conceptually simple but wasn't popularized until **Rumelhart, Hinton & Williams (1986)** — a roughly **17-year gap**.
- Lesson: limitations of a *specific* model (a single layer) were over-generalized to the whole paradigm.

notes.heading: Choosing Activations & Output Layers (Cheat Sheet)
notes.body:
- **Hidden layers:** default to **ReLU** (or Leaky ReLU/GELU/ELU variants) — cheap, non-saturating, avoids vanishing gradients.
- **Binary classification output:** **sigmoid** $\to$ one probability in $(0,1)$, trained with **binary cross-entropy**.
- **Multi-class output:** **softmax**, $\;\text{softmax}(z)_i=\dfrac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}$, trained with **categorical cross-entropy** $\;\mathcal{L}=-\sum_i y_i\log\hat y_i$.
- **Regression output:** **linear/identity** activation.
- Avoid **sigmoid/tanh in deep hidden layers** (vanishing gradients); avoid using only linear hidden layers with a single end non-linearity (the linear part collapses to one linear map).

quiz.q: Why is stacking 100 linear layers (no activations) equivalent to a single linear layer?
options: Because the gradients vanish after 100 layers, deleting the extra layers
Because composing linear maps yields a linear map, so the product of all weight matrices is itself one matrix
Because the biases cancel out across layers
Because each layer randomly reinitializes, undoing the previous one
quiz.explanation: Composing linear maps gives another linear map: $W_{100}\cdots W_1 x = W_{\text{eff}}x$ (this relies on associativity of matrix multiplication), so the whole stack collapses to one effective matrix and one effective bias. Vanishing gradients (option A) is a separate training issue, not the reason for the collapse, and biases do not cancel — they combine into one effective bias.

quiz.q: A single neuron with a sigmoid activation is mathematically equivalent to which classical model?
options: Linear regression
k-nearest neighbors
Logistic regression
A decision tree
quiz.explanation: Weighted sum + bias + sigmoid is exactly logistic regression — the same model with a 'neuron' name. Linear regression has no sigmoid squashing; k-NN and decision trees are non-parametric and unrelated to the $\sigma(w^\top x+b)$ form.

quiz.q: Why can a single neuron NOT solve the XOR problem?
options: XOR has too many input features for one neuron
XOR's four points are not linearly separable, and a single neuron only draws a straight boundary
Sigmoid cannot represent the value 0
XOR requires negative weights, which a perceptron cannot have
quiz.explanation: A single neuron produces one linear decision boundary ($w^\top x+b=0$). The XOR points cannot be separated by any single straight line (non-linearly separable), so one neuron fails. XOR has only 2 inputs; sigmoid and negative weights are both fine — those distractors are false.

quiz.q: Which activation is the standard default for hidden layers, and why?
options: Sigmoid, because it outputs a clean probability
Tanh, because it is zero-centered and bounded
ReLU, because it is cheap and its gradient is 1 for positive inputs, avoiding vanishing gradients
Softmax, because it normalizes the layer outputs
quiz.explanation: ReLU ($\max(0,z)$) has derivative 1 for $z>0$, so it does not shrink gradients on the active path and is computationally cheap — making it the hidden-layer default. Sigmoid/tanh saturate and cause vanishing gradients; softmax is an output activation for multi-class probabilities, not a hidden-layer choice.

quiz.q: The sigmoid derivative $\sigma'(z)=\sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z))$ has a maximum value of:
quiz.explanation: $\sigma'(z)$ is maximized when $\sigma(z)=0.5$ (i.e. $z=0$), giving $0.5\times0.5=0.25$. This $\le 0.25$ cap means each sigmoid layer shrinks the backpropagated gradient by at least $4\times$, which contributes to vanishing gradients in deep nets.

quiz.q: In a 2-2-1 MLP that solves XOR, what is the role of the hidden layer?
options: It increases the number of output classes
It re-represents the inputs so the problem becomes linearly separable for the output neuron
It normalizes the inputs to zero mean
It memorizes the four training points without generalizing
quiz.explanation: The two hidden neurons each learn a line (e.g. OR-like and NAND-like). They transform the inputs into a new feature space where XOR becomes linearly separable, so the output neuron can combine them with an AND-like operation. This representation learning — not normalization or memorization — is why the hidden layer works.

quiz.q: What is the primary consequence of vanishing gradients in a deep sigmoid network?
options: The output layer overfits while hidden layers stay perfect
Early (input-side) layers receive tiny gradients and learn very slowly or not at all
The loss becomes negative
The network's forward pass produces NaNs
quiz.explanation: Backprop multiplies many small saturated derivatives together; the product shrinks exponentially with depth, so the earliest layers get near-zero gradient and barely update. It is a learning/training problem, not a negative-loss or NaN problem, and it harms early layers most, not just the output.

quiz.q: For a 3-class mutually-exclusive classification problem, the correct output-layer activation is:
options: Sigmoid on a single neuron
ReLU on 3 neurons
Softmax on 3 neurons
Tanh on 3 neurons
quiz.explanation: Softmax over 3 output neurons produces a normalized probability distribution summing to 1, the right choice for mutually exclusive multi-class classification. A single sigmoid is for binary; ReLU/tanh do not produce a normalized probability distribution.

quiz.q: What does increasing the depth of an MLP intuitively allow, per the 'lines → shapes → anything' framing?
options: One layer draws arbitrary curves; more layers just speed training
One layer draws lines; two layers compose regions/shapes; three+ can approximate essentially any boundary
Depth only reduces the number of parameters
Depth removes the need for activation functions
quiz.explanation: A single layer carves linear boundaries; stacking layers (with non-linear activations) composes them into curved regions and increasingly complex decision surfaces. Depth needs activations to add expressivity — it does not remove them — and it generally adds parameters rather than removing them.

quiz.q: Why was Minsky & Papert's 1969 result so influential to the field, even though MLPs were the simple fix?
options: They proved no neural network could ever learn anything

They highlighted that the field's core building block (the single-layer perceptron) failed on a trivial problem, so many researchers concluded the approach was a dead end before backprop was popularized
They banned funding for AI research worldwide
They proved that XOR is uncomputable
quiz.explanation: Their analysis targeted the single-layer perceptron's failure on XOR; many researchers over-generalized this to the whole paradigm and abandoned it. The conceptually simple fix (hidden layers + backpropagation) was not popularized until 1986 — a ~17-year gap. They did not prove all NNs useless, nor that XOR is uncomputable (XOR is trivially computable).

quiz.q: Suppose you replace every ReLU in a deep MLP with the identity function $f(z)=z$. What happens?
options: Training becomes faster but accuracy is unchanged
The network collapses to an equivalent single linear (affine) layer and can no longer fit non-linear data
The vanishing-gradient problem gets worse
The network can now solve XOR more easily
quiz.explanation: Identity activations are linear, so the whole stack reduces to one affine transformation $W_{\text{eff}}x+b_{\text{eff}}$ — it loses all non-linear modeling power and cannot solve XOR. It does not improve XOR or merely speed things up; the issue is loss of expressivity, not gradient magnitude.

quiz.q: What property makes tanh often preferable to sigmoid for hidden units (when one of them must be used)?
options: Tanh never saturates
Tanh is zero-centered, with outputs in $(-1,1)$, which tends to improve optimization
Tanh has a constant derivative of 1
Tanh outputs valid probabilities
quiz.explanation: Tanh maps to $(-1,1)$ and is zero-centered, which keeps activations balanced around 0 and helps gradient flow relative to sigmoid's $(0,1)$ output. It still saturates (so its derivative is not constant and not non-vanishing — it peaks at 1 only at $z=0$), and it does not output probabilities — that is sigmoid/softmax.

descriptive.q: Explain why activation functions are essential in an MLP, and prove that without them a deep network is no more powerful than a single linear (affine) layer.
descriptive.answer:
**Activation functions inject non-linearity**, which is what allows a network to bend decision boundaries and approximate complex functions. Without them, the network is restricted to affine maps regardless of depth.

**Proof of collapse.** Let layer $\ell$ compute $a^{(\ell)} = W^{(\ell)}a^{(\ell-1)} + b^{(\ell)}$ with no activation. Then for two layers:
$$a^{(2)} = W^{(2)}\big(W^{(1)}x + b^{(1)}\big)+b^{(2)} = \underbrace{(W^{(2)}W^{(1)})}_{W_{\text{eff}}}x + \underbrace{(W^{(2)}b^{(1)}+b^{(2)})}_{b_{\text{eff}}}.$$
By induction over $L$ layers, $a^{(L)} = W_{\text{eff}}\,x + b_{\text{eff}}$ where $W_{\text{eff}}=W^{(L)}\cdots W^{(1)}$. Because matrix multiplication is **associative**, the entire stack reduces to a single affine transformation — exactly one linear layer.

**Consequence.** Such a network can only represent linear decision boundaries and cannot solve non-linearly-separable problems like XOR. Inserting a non-linear $\sigma$ between layers breaks the product chain, so each added layer can genuinely add modeling power.

descriptive.q: Compare sigmoid, tanh, and ReLU as activation functions. When would you use each, and what are their failure modes?
descriptive.answer:
**Sigmoid** $\sigma(z)=\frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}\in(0,1)$: smooth, interpretable as a probability. *Use:* output layer of **binary classification**. *Failure mode:* saturates for large $|z|$, derivative $\le 0.25$, **not zero-centered** → **vanishing gradients** and slow learning in deep hidden layers.

**Tanh** $\tanh(z)\in(-1,1)$: like sigmoid but **zero-centered**, which helps optimization. *Use:* hidden layers in shallow nets or RNNs when a bounded activation is wanted. *Failure mode:* still **saturates** at the extremes → vanishing gradients in deep stacks.

**ReLU** $\max(0,z)$: derivative $1$ for $z>0$, $0$ otherwise. *Use:* **default for hidden layers** — cheap, non-saturating on the positive side, mitigates vanishing gradients, induces sparsity. *Failure mode:* **dying ReLU** — neurons that always output $0$ get zero gradient and never recover; addressed by **Leaky ReLU** $\max(\alpha z, z)$, ELU, or GELU.

**Rule of thumb:** ReLU (or a variant) for hidden layers; sigmoid for binary output; softmax for multi-class output; linear/identity for regression output.

descriptive.q: Walk through, step by step, how a 2-2-1 MLP solves the XOR problem. What does each neuron learn, and why does the hidden layer make the problem solvable?
descriptive.answer:
The XOR truth table is $0\oplus0=0,\;0\oplus1=1,\;1\oplus0=1,\;1\oplus1=0$. Plotting the four points, the two positive points $(0,1),(1,0)$ and two negative points $(0,0),(1,1)$ **cannot be separated by any single straight line** — XOR is not linearly separable.

**Hidden layer (representation learning).** Each of the 2 hidden neurons learns its own line:
- Hidden neuron $h_1$ learns an **OR-like** boundary: fires when at least one input is 1.
- Hidden neuron $h_2$ learns a **NAND-like** boundary: fires unless both inputs are 1.

**Output neuron (combination).** XOR can be written $\text{XOR}= \text{OR} \;\text{AND}\; \text{NAND}$. The output neuron applies an **AND-like** operation to $h_1$ and $h_2$: it fires only when OR is true *and* NAND is true — i.e. exactly when one (but not both) input is 1.

**Why it works.** The hidden layer **re-represents** the inputs in a new feature space $(h_1,h_2)$ in which the four points *are* linearly separable. The output neuron then needs only one linear boundary in that transformed space. This is the essence of depth: hidden layers learn features that make the final classification linear — *why 'deep' learning is deep.*

descriptive.q: Explain why vanishing gradients arise with sigmoid activations in deep networks, and explain why ReLU largely avoids this.
descriptive.answer:
During backpropagation, the gradient of the loss w.r.t. an early layer's pre-activation is a **product of per-layer Jacobian factors**, each containing the activation derivative. Schematically, for a chain of layers,
$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z^{(1)}} \;\propto\; \prod_{\ell} \big(W^{(\ell)}\big)^\top \,\mathrm{diag}\!\big(\sigma'(z^{(\ell)})\big).$$
For **sigmoid**, $\sigma'(z)=\sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z)) \le 0.25$, and it is $\approx 0$ whenever a unit is saturated ($|z|$ large). Multiplying many factors $\le 0.25$ together makes the activation-derivative product shrink **exponentially with depth**:
$$\Big|\prod_\ell \sigma'(z^{(\ell)})\Big| \le 0.25^{L} \to 0.$$
So early layers receive a vanishingly small gradient and barely learn.

**ReLU.** Its derivative is exactly $1$ for active units ($z>0$) and $0$ otherwise. Along the active path, the product of activation derivatives is $1^{L}=1$ — gradients are **not shrunk** by the activation. This preserves gradient magnitude through depth (the main reason ReLU is the default), at the cost of the dying-ReLU issue when units fall permanently into the $z<0$ region.

descriptive.q: Why was Minsky & Papert's 1969 perceptron result historically influential, even though the fix (MLPs) was conceptually simple? What is the broader lesson?
descriptive.answer:
Minsky & Papert rigorously analyzed the limits of the single-layer perceptron, showing it cannot represent functions that are not linearly separable, with **XOR** as the canonical counterexample. Because the perceptron was regarded as the **fundamental building block** of neural networks, the result was widely read as a verdict on the entire approach: many researchers concluded that neural nets were a **dead end**, funding and interest declined, and the field entered an **'AI winter.'**

The fix — adding **hidden layers** and training them with **backpropagation** — is conceptually simple, but it was not effectively developed and popularized until **Rumelhart, Hinton & Williams (1986)**, roughly a **17-year gap**. By then most researchers had moved on to symbolic AI and other methods.

**Broader lesson:** a proven limitation of one *specific* model (the single layer) was **over-generalized** to a whole paradigm. The capability ceiling was an artifact of restricting to a single layer, not an inherent limit of neural computation. It is a cautionary tale about distinguishing 'this particular architecture can't' from 'this whole idea can't.'

descriptive.q: How does the depth of an MLP relate to the complexity of decision boundaries it can represent? Connect this to the Universal Approximation Theorem.
descriptive.answer:
Intuitively: **one layer = lines** (linear boundaries), **two layers = shapes/regions** (intersections and unions of half-spaces, producing convex and then more complex regions), and **three or more layers = essentially any boundary**. Each added layer composes the previous layer's features into more intricate, curved decision surfaces.

**Universal Approximation Theorem (UAT).** A feedforward network with a **single hidden layer** of sufficiently many neurons and a suitable non-linear activation can approximate **any continuous function on a compact set** to arbitrary accuracy. So in principle even one hidden layer is enough for universal approximation.

**Why go deeper, then?** UAT is an *existence* result — it says nothing about *how many* neurons are needed or whether training will actually find the solution. **Depth is often dramatically more parameter-efficient**: deep networks can represent certain functions with far fewer neurons than a shallow network would require, and the hierarchical features they learn tend to generalize better. The non-linearity is essential in both cases — without it, no amount of width or depth escapes the linear regime.

descriptive.q: You must classify data with a binary target. Specify and justify the activation choices for hidden layers and the output layer, and the matching loss function.
descriptive.answer:
**Hidden layers:** use **ReLU** (or a variant like Leaky ReLU). Justification: ReLU's gradient is 1 for active units so it avoids the **vanishing gradient** problem that sigmoid/tanh cause in deeper hidden layers, it is computationally cheap, and it trains faster.

**Output layer:** use a single **sigmoid** neuron. Justification: sigmoid squashes the logit into $(0,1)$, interpretable as $P(y=1\mid x)$, the natural output for binary classification.

**Loss:** **binary cross-entropy** (log loss):
$$\mathcal{L} = -\big[\,y\log\hat y + (1-y)\log(1-\hat y)\,\big],\qquad \hat y=\sigma(w^\top a + b).$$
This loss pairs cleanly with sigmoid: the gradient of this loss w.r.t. the logit simplifies to $\hat y - y$, giving well-behaved, non-saturating updates. Using sigmoid in the **hidden** layers instead would risk vanishing gradients; using a linear output with cross-entropy, or squared error with sigmoid, would be ill-matched and slower to optimize.

descriptive.q: What is the 'dying ReLU' problem, why does it occur, and what mitigations address it? Contrast with the failure mode of sigmoid.
descriptive.answer:
**Dying ReLU** happens when a neuron's pre-activation $z$ becomes negative for essentially all inputs. Since $\mathrm{ReLU}(z)=0$ and its derivative is $0$ for $z<0$, that neuron outputs 0 and receives **zero gradient**, so its weights never update — it is effectively **dead** and contributes nothing. This is typically triggered by a **large learning rate** or a large negative bias push that knocks the unit into the negative region permanently.

**Mitigations:**
- **Leaky ReLU** $\max(\alpha z, z)$ (small $\alpha$, e.g. 0.01) keeps a small non-zero gradient for $z<0$ so units can recover.
- **Parametric ReLU (PReLU)** learns $\alpha$; **ELU/GELU** give smooth negative-side responses.
- Careful **initialization** (e.g. He init) and **smaller/clipped learning rates**.

**Contrast with sigmoid.** Sigmoid's failure is **vanishing gradients from saturation**: when $|z|$ is large the derivative $\to 0$ at *both* extremes, shrinking gradients throughout deep networks (but the unit still passes some signal). Dying ReLU is a sparser, more binary failure — specific units go permanently silent — whereas sigmoid saturation is a graded, network-wide gradient-shrinkage problem.

topics.title: Backpropagation
topics.summary: This session covers backpropagation: the algorithm that lets neural networks learn by flowing gradients backward through the network to update every weight. It builds from the chain rule to computational graphs (DAGs) to the full forward-loss-backward-update loop, and examines gradient flow including the vanishing/exploding gradient problem and why ReLU helps. PyTorch autograd automates the backward pass via loss.backward().

notes.heading: The Chain Rule: Global Gradients from Local Ones
notes.body:
Backprop's core idea: a hard global derivative is a **product of easy local derivatives**. If the loss depends on weight $w$ through a chain $w \to z \to h \to \hat y \to L$, then
$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial w} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat y}\cdot\frac{\partial \hat y}{\partial h}\cdot\frac{\partial h}{\partial z}\cdot\frac{\partial z}{\partial w}.$$
- **Single-variable:** $\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{dy}{du}\cdot\frac{du}{dx}$.
- **Multivariable (fan-out):** if a node feeds several downstream paths, **sum** the contributions: $\frac{\partial L}{\partial x} = \sum_i \frac{\partial L}{\partial u_i}\frac{\partial u_i}{\partial x}$.
- Each factor is a **local gradient** computed at one operation — simple and reusable. The chain rule is what connects the scalar loss to **every** parameter through layers of composition.

notes.heading: Computational Graphs (DAGs)
notes.body:
Any computation is drawn as a **directed acyclic graph**: **nodes = operations**, **edges = data flow**.
- **Forward pass:** evaluate left to right, **store every intermediate value** (needed later).
- **Backward pass:** propagate gradients right to left; at each node multiply **upstream gradient $\times$ local gradient**.
- Worked example $f(x,y)=(x+y)(x-y)$: let $p=x+y,\; q=x-y,\; f=pq$. Then $\frac{\partial f}{\partial p}=q$, $\frac{\partial f}{\partial q}=p$, and $\frac{\partial f}{\partial x}=q\cdot 1 + p\cdot 1 = (x-y)+(x+y)=2x$, $\frac{\partial f}{\partial y}=q\cdot 1 + p\cdot(-1)=(x-y)-(x+y)=-2y$. At $x=3,y=1$: $\partial f/\partial x=6$, $\partial f/\partial y=-2$. (Equivalently $f=x^2-y^2$.)
- Each node is **local/independent** — it only needs its inputs and the upstream gradient. This locality is why backprop **scales to billions of parameters**.

notes.heading: Why Forward Values Are Stored
notes.body:
Local gradients often **depend on the forward-pass values**, so they must be cached.
- **Multiply** $f=a\cdot b$: $\frac{\partial f}{\partial a}=b$, $\frac{\partial f}{\partial b}=a$ — each derivative is the **other input's value**.
- **Add** $f=a+b$: gradients are $1$ and $1$ — addition just **routes/copies** the upstream gradient to both inputs.
- **Max / ReLU** $f=\max(0,z)$: gradient is $1$ if $z>0$ else $0$ — it **gates** (routes the gradient only to the active branch).
- Consequence: you cannot run the backward pass without the forward pass; this is the memory cost of training (activations are kept until the backward pass uses them).

notes.heading: The Backpropagation Algorithm (4 Steps)
notes.body:
The same loop trains **every architecture**:
1. **Forward pass** — compute the prediction $\hat y$ (store intermediates).
2. **Compute loss** $L$ (e.g. MSE $\tfrac12(\hat y-y)^2$, or cross-entropy).
3. **Backward pass** — compute $\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial w}$ for **every** weight via the chain rule.
4. **Update** weights with gradient descent:
$$w_{\text{new}} = w_{\text{old}} - \eta\,\frac{\partial L}{\partial w}.$$
Repeat. Here $\eta$ is the **learning rate**. Backprop computes gradients; the **optimizer** (SGD, Adam, ...) consumes them to update weights — they are distinct steps.

notes.heading: Sign and Magnitude of Gradients (Intuition)
notes.body:
For MSE $L=\tfrac12(\hat y - y)^2$, $\frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat y}=(\hat y - y)$.
- **Example:** $\hat y = 0.594$, target $y=1.0 \Rightarrow \hat y - y = -0.406 < 0$, so the output is **too low**.
- A **negative** $\partial L/\partial w$ means increasing $w$ **decreases** the loss; the update $w-\eta\,\partial L/\partial w$ therefore **increases** $w$, pushing $\hat y$ **up toward the target**.
- This is why "all gradients negative" is consistent with an under-shooting prediction. The **sign** tells gradient descent which direction to move; the **magnitude** tells how strongly that weight affects the loss.

notes.heading: Vanishing & Exploding Gradients
notes.body:
Backprop multiplies many local gradients along a deep chain, so magnitudes **compound**.
- **Sigmoid** $\sigma(z)=\frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ has derivative $\sigma'(z)=\sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z))$ with **maximum $0.25$** (at $z=0$).
- After 10 sigmoid layers: $0.25^{10}\approx 10^{-6}$; after 20 layers: $0.25^{20}\approx 10^{-12}$ — gradients **vanish**, so early layers barely update and stay near their **random initialization**.
- **Exploding** gradients are the mirror image: local factors $>1$ multiply up to huge values, causing unstable/NaN updates (mitigated by **gradient clipping**, careful init).
- Both are consequences of repeated multiplication, controlled by activation choice, weight initialization (e.g. Xavier/He), and normalization.

notes.heading: Why ReLU Fixes Vanishing Gradients
notes.body:
**ReLU** $f(z)=\max(0,z)$ has derivative
$$f'(z)=\begin{cases}1 & z>0\\ 0 & z<0\end{cases}$$
(at $z=0$ it is non-differentiable; a subgradient of $0$ is used in practice).
- For active units the local gradient is exactly **1**, so a chain gives $1^{10}=1$ — gradients **flow unchanged** through depth instead of shrinking.
- This non-saturating behavior (for positive inputs) is a key reason **ReLU enabled deep learning**.
- Trade-off: **dying ReLU** — units stuck at $z\le 0$ get zero gradient forever; variants like **Leaky ReLU** / ELU / GELU give a small/non-zero negative-side slope to mitigate this.

notes.heading: PyTorch Autograd
notes.body:
PyTorch builds the computational graph **dynamically** during the forward pass (define-by-run) and automates the backward pass.
- Tensors created with **requires_grad=True** are tracked; each operation records its inputs and a local backward rule.
- **loss.backward()** traverses the graph in **reverse topological order**, applying the chain rule to compute $\partial L/\partial w$ for every tracked parameter.
- Results are **accumulated** (added) into each tensor's **.grad** attribute (hence the need to zero gradients between steps).
- This is the same idea as a tiny hand-built autograd (e.g. a Value class / micrograd): store inputs plus a backward function per op, then walk the graph backward.

quiz.q: In the computational graph for $f = a \cdot b$, what is $\frac{\partial f}{\partial a}$, and why must the forward pass run first?
options: $\frac{\partial f}{\partial a}=a$; we need $a$'s value from the forward pass
$\frac{\partial f}{\partial a}=b$; we need $b$'s value, which was stored during the forward pass
$\frac{\partial f}{\partial a}=1$; multiplication just copies the gradient
$\frac{\partial f}{\partial a}=ab$; we need both stored values
quiz.explanation: For $f=ab$, $\partial f/\partial a=b$ — the derivative w.r.t. one input is the value of the OTHER input. Since that value is only known after the forward pass, intermediates must be stored. Choice 0 swaps the inputs; choice 2 is the rule for addition, not multiplication.

quiz.q: A network has 20 sigmoid hidden layers. Using the maximum sigmoid derivative, the best-case gradient reaching the first layer is about:
options: $0.25^{20}\approx 10^{-12}$ — essentially zero
$0.5^{20}\approx 10^{-6}$ — small but trainable
$0.25\times 20 = 5$ — gradients explode
$1^{20}=1$ — gradients flow unchanged
quiz.explanation: The sigmoid derivative peaks at $0.25$ (at $z=0$), and backprop multiplies it across layers: $0.25^{20}\approx10^{-12}$. The first layer effectively cannot learn (vanishing gradients). The correct max is $0.25$, not $0.5$ (choice 1 uses the wrong base), and the factors multiply, not add (choice 2).

quiz.q: For MSE loss with $\hat y = 0.594$ and target $y = 1.0$, all weight gradients come out negative. What does the optimizer do and why is it correct?
options: It decreases the weights, because negative gradients mean the weights are too large
It increases the weights, because $w-\eta\,\partial L/\partial w$ with negative gradient raises $w$, pushing $\hat y$ up toward 1.0
It leaves the weights unchanged, because the loss is already minimized
It flips the sign of the loss before updating
quiz.explanation: $\hat y - y = -0.406 < 0$ means the output is too low. A negative $\partial L/\partial w$ means increasing $w$ lowers the loss; the update $w-\eta\cdot(\text{negative})$ increases $w$, raising $\hat y$ toward the target. Choice 0 has the direction backward — subtracting a negative number adds.

quiz.q: At an addition node $f = a + b$, how does the backward pass treat the incoming (upstream) gradient $g$?
options: It splits $g$ in half, sending $g/2$ to each input
It multiplies $g$ by the other input's value
It routes $g$ unchanged to both $a$ and $b$ (local gradients are 1)
It sends $g$ to $a$ and $-g$ to $b$
quiz.explanation: For $f=a+b$, $\partial f/\partial a=\partial f/\partial b=1$, so addition acts as a gradient distributor — copying $g$ to each input. It does not halve (choice 0, a common misconception), nor use input values (that is multiplication), nor negate (that would be subtraction $a-b$).

quiz.q: For $f(x,y)=(x+y)(x-y)$ at $x=3,\,y=1$, the gradients $\partial f/\partial x$ and $\partial f/\partial y$ are:
quiz.explanation: $f=x^2-y^2$, so $\partial f/\partial x=2x=6$ and $\partial f/\partial y=-2y=-2$. Via the graph, $x$ fans out to both $p=x+y$ and $q=x-y$, so its gradient sums two paths: $q\cdot1+p\cdot1=2x$. Choice 1 forgets the fan-out summation.

quiz.q: Why does ReLU help gradients flow through deep networks better than sigmoid?
options: ReLU has a derivative of exactly 1 for positive inputs, so chains of them give $1^n=1$ instead of shrinking
ReLU's derivative is always greater than 1, amplifying the gradient
ReLU is a smooth, bounded function so gradients never change
ReLU squashes outputs to $[0,1]$, keeping gradients stable
quiz.explanation: For $z>0$, ReLU's derivative is exactly 1, so multiplying many of them keeps the gradient unchanged ($1^{10}=1$), avoiding the decay sigmoid causes. Choice 1 is false (it is 1, not $>1$); choice 3 confuses ReLU with sigmoid, which is the function that squashes to $[0,1]$ and causes vanishing gradients.

quiz.q: What is the correct order of the four backpropagation training steps?
options: Backward pass → forward pass → compute loss → update weights
Forward pass → compute loss → backward pass → update weights
Compute loss → forward pass → update weights → backward pass
Forward pass → update weights → compute loss → backward pass
quiz.explanation: You must compute the prediction (forward), measure error (loss), compute gradients (backward), then apply the update. You cannot compute a loss before a prediction, nor compute gradients before a loss exists, ruling out the other orders.

quiz.q: In PyTorch, calling loss.backward() does what, and where do results go?
options: It performs the weight update; results are written to each tensor's .data
It computes $\partial L/\partial w$ for tracked params by traversing the graph in reverse; results accumulate in each tensor's .grad
It runs the forward pass again to recompute the loss; results go to a new tensor
It zeros all gradients to prepare for the next iteration
quiz.explanation: backward() applies the chain rule in reverse topological order over the dynamic graph, storing gradients in .grad for every tensor with requires_grad=True. The actual update is the optimizer's job (choice 0), and gradients accumulate, which is why we explicitly zero them (choice 3 describes zero_grad, not backward).

quiz.q: A node $x$ feeds into TWO downstream operations $u_1$ and $u_2$. The correct backward rule for $\partial L/\partial x$ is:
options: Take the maximum of the two path gradients
Multiply the two path gradients together
Sum the contributions: $\frac{\partial L}{\partial u_1}\frac{\partial u_1}{\partial x}+\frac{\partial L}{\partial u_2}\frac{\partial u_2}{\partial x}$
Use only the first path and ignore the second
quiz.explanation: The multivariable chain rule says gradients along multiple paths ADD (because the total change in $L$ is the sum of changes through each path). Multiplying (choice 1) or taking a max/single path discards real contributions and gives wrong gradients.

quiz.q: What primarily causes the EXPLODING gradient problem in deep networks?
options: Repeated multiplication of local gradients whose magnitudes exceed 1, compounding to very large values
Using ReLU, whose derivative is always greater than 1
Too small a learning rate, which slows learning
Storing too many forward-pass intermediates in memory
quiz.explanation: Exploding gradients are the mirror of vanishing: when chained local factors are $>1$, their product grows exponentially with depth, giving huge (possibly NaN) updates; gradient clipping helps. ReLU's derivative is at most 1 (choice 1 is false), and learning rate or memory (choices 2, 3) are unrelated to the compounding cause.

quiz.q: Why is each node in a computational graph said to be 'local' (independent), and why does this matter?
options: Because each node only needs its inputs and the upstream gradient to compute its local gradient, which lets backprop scale to billions of parameters
Because nodes never share data, so the graph has no edges
Because each node computes the full global gradient on its own
Because nodes can be evaluated in any random order during the forward pass
quiz.explanation: A node needs only its stored inputs and the gradient arriving from downstream; it does not need global knowledge. This locality/modularity is exactly what makes backprop tractable at massive scale. Edges do exist (choice 1); no single node computes the global gradient (choice 2); and forward order must respect the DAG topology (choice 3).

quiz.q: A unit's input stays at $z \le 0$ for all training examples under ReLU. What happens during backprop, and what is this called?
options: The gradient through it is 1, so it learns normally
The gradient through it is 0, so its weights never update — the 'dying ReLU' problem
The gradient explodes because $\max(0,z)$ is undefined
The gradient is split among neighboring units
quiz.explanation: For $z\le 0$, ReLU's derivative is 0, so no gradient flows and the unit's weights are frozen — the dying ReLU problem, mitigated by Leaky ReLU/ELU/GELU. The derivative is 0 there, not 1 (choice 0); nothing is undefined in practice (a subgradient of 0 is used).

descriptive.q: State the chain rule and explain why it is the foundation of backpropagation. Use the chain $w\to z\to h\to\hat y\to L$ to illustrate.
descriptive.answer:
The **chain rule** says that the derivative of a composition is the product of the derivatives of its parts. For the chain $w\to z\to h\to\hat y\to L$:
$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial w}=\frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat y}\cdot\frac{\partial \hat y}{\partial h}\cdot\frac{\partial h}{\partial z}\cdot\frac{\partial z}{\partial w}.$$
Each factor is a **local gradient** — the derivative of one operation with respect to its immediate input — which is typically trivial to compute (e.g. for $z=Wx+b$, $\partial z/\partial W=x$).

This is foundational because it turns one **hard global problem** (how does a weight buried deep in the network affect the final scalar loss?) into a **product of many easy local problems**. Backprop simply walks the network backward, and at each step multiplies the gradient arriving from downstream (the **upstream gradient**) by the node's local gradient. When a value fans out to several downstream paths, the contributions are **summed** (multivariable chain rule). Without the chain rule, we would have no systematic way to attribute the loss to individual parameters.

descriptive.q: Walk through the forward and backward pass for $f(x,y)=(x+y)(x-y)$ at $x=3,\,y=1$, drawing out the computational graph and showing how the fan-out of $x$ is handled.
descriptive.answer:
**Graph / forward pass.** Define intermediates: $p=x+y$ and $q=x-y$, then $f=p\cdot q$. At $x=3,y=1$: $p=4$, $q=2$, $f=8$. (Sanity check: $f=x^2-y^2=9-1=8$.) Note $x$ **fans out** — it is an input to both $p$ and $q$.

**Backward pass.** Start with $\partial f/\partial f=1$.
- At the multiply node: $\partial f/\partial p=q=2$ and $\partial f/\partial q=p=4$ (each is the *other* input's stored value).
- Local gradients of the add/subtract nodes: $\partial p/\partial x=1,\ \partial p/\partial y=1,\ \partial q/\partial x=1,\ \partial q/\partial y=-1$.

Now combine. Because $x$ feeds **two** paths, **sum** them:
$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x}=\frac{\partial f}{\partial p}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}+\frac{\partial f}{\partial q}\frac{\partial q}{\partial x}=2\cdot 1+4\cdot 1=6.$$
$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial y}=\frac{\partial f}{\partial p}\frac{\partial p}{\partial y}+\frac{\partial f}{\partial q}\frac{\partial q}{\partial y}=2\cdot 1+4\cdot(-1)=-2.$$
These match the closed form $\partial f/\partial x=2x=6$ and $\partial f/\partial y=-2y=-2$. The key lesson: **fan-out in the forward pass becomes a sum of gradients in the backward pass.**

descriptive.q: Why must the forward pass store intermediate values? Contrast how the add, multiply, and ReLU nodes use (or don't use) stored values during backprop.
descriptive.answer:
Backprop needs **local gradients**, and for many operations those gradients are functions of the forward-pass values, so the values must be cached. This is the memory cost of training: activations are held until the backward pass consumes them.

- **Multiply** $f=a\cdot b$: $\partial f/\partial a=b$, $\partial f/\partial b=a$. The gradient with respect to one input is the **stored value of the other input** — so both forward values are required.
- **Add** $f=a+b$: $\partial f/\partial a=\partial f/\partial b=1$. Local gradients are constant, so the node needs **no stored values** — it simply **routes/copies** the upstream gradient to both inputs.
- **ReLU** $f=\max(0,z)$: $\partial f/\partial z=\mathbb{1}[z>0]$. It needs to know the **sign of the stored pre-activation $z$** (or equivalently whether the output was active) to decide whether to pass the gradient (factor 1) or block it (factor 0). So ReLU **gates** the gradient based on a stored value.

This is why you cannot run the backward pass without first running the forward pass — the graph's intermediate tensors are exactly what the local gradient rules read.

descriptive.q: Explain the vanishing gradient problem quantitatively for a deep sigmoid network, and explain precisely why switching the hidden activation to ReLU helps.
descriptive.answer:
**Sigmoid case.** The sigmoid $\sigma(z)=\frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ has derivative $\sigma'(z)=\sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z))$, which is maximized at $z=0$ with value $0.25$ and is smaller everywhere else (it saturates toward 0 for large $|z|$). In backprop, the gradient reaching an early layer is a **product** of one such factor per layer. With $L$ layers the best case is $0.25^{L}$: for $L=10$ that is $\approx 10^{-6}$, and for $L=20$ it is $0.25^{20}\approx 10^{-12}$ — effectively zero. The practical consequence is that early layers receive almost no gradient, so their weights stay near their **random initialization** and never learn useful features. This is the **vanishing gradient problem**.

**Why ReLU helps.** ReLU's derivative is exactly **1** for positive inputs (and 0 otherwise). For active units the chained product is $1^{L}=1$, so gradients **flow through depth unchanged** rather than decaying geometrically. ReLU is also non-saturating on the positive side, so it does not flatten out for large activations. This is a major reason ReLU **enabled training of deep networks**. The caveat is the **dying ReLU** problem: a unit stuck at $z\le0$ gets zero gradient permanently, motivating Leaky ReLU / ELU / GELU, which keep a small non-zero slope on the negative side.

descriptive.q: A network predicts $\hat y = 0.594$ for a target $y = 1.0$ under MSE loss, and every weight gradient is negative. Explain intuitively and mathematically why negative gradients are exactly what we expect, and trace what the update does.
descriptive.answer:
**Mathematically.** For MSE $L=\tfrac12(\hat y-y)^2$, the gradient at the output is $\frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat y}=\hat y-y=0.594-1.0=-0.406<0$. Every weight gradient is this term times a chain of local gradients back to that weight; if those chains are positive, the overall $\partial L/\partial w$ inherits the **negative sign**.

**Intuitively.** The prediction is **below** the target — the network is undershooting. A negative $\partial L/\partial w$ means: *increasing this weight would decrease the loss.* So the right thing to do is increase the weight.

**The update.** Gradient descent does $w_{\text{new}}=w_{\text{old}}-\eta\,\frac{\partial L}{\partial w}$. With $\partial L/\partial w<0$, subtracting a negative number **increases** $w$. Larger weights raise the pre-activations and push $\hat y$ **up toward 1.0**, reducing the loss on the next forward pass. So the negative gradients, combined with the minus sign in the update rule, correctly move the output in the direction that closes the gap. The general principle: the **sign** of the gradient tells descent which way to move each weight, and its **magnitude** tells how strongly that weight influences the loss.

descriptive.q: List the four steps of the backpropagation/training loop in order, and explain why backpropagation and the optimizer (weight update) are conceptually distinct.
descriptive.answer:
**The four steps (one iteration):**
1. **Forward pass** — feed the input through the network to compute the prediction $\hat y$, storing all intermediate activations.
2. **Compute the loss** $L$ — measure the error between $\hat y$ and the target $y$ (e.g. MSE or cross-entropy).
3. **Backward pass** — apply the chain rule through the computational graph (right to left) to compute $\partial L/\partial w$ for **every** trainable parameter.
4. **Update** — adjust each weight: $w_{\text{new}}=w_{\text{old}}-\eta\,\partial L/\partial w$. Then repeat.

**Why backprop is not the optimizer.** *Backpropagation* is purely a **gradient-computation** algorithm: given the graph and the loss, it produces $\partial L/\partial w$ — nothing more. The *optimizer* is a separate component that **decides how to use those gradients** to change the weights. Plain SGD applies step 4 directly, but Adam, RMSProp, momentum, etc. transform the raw gradients (using running averages, per-parameter scaling, etc.) before stepping. Keeping them separate is powerful: you can swap optimizers without changing how gradients are computed, and the same backprop machinery serves any update rule. In PyTorch this maps cleanly to loss.backward() (fills .grad) followed by optimizer.step() (applies the update).

descriptive.q: Describe how PyTorch autograd works, what loss.backward() does, and why gradients accumulate in .grad (and what that implies for the training loop).
descriptive.answer:
PyTorch autograd builds a **dynamic computational graph** on the fly during the forward pass. Any tensor created with **requires_grad=True** is tracked; each operation it participates in records its inputs and a corresponding **local backward function**. The graph is therefore reconstructed each iteration (define-by-run), which makes it flexible for data-dependent control flow.

Calling **loss.backward()** starts from the scalar loss with gradient 1 and traverses the graph in **reverse topological order**, applying each node's backward function — i.e. multiplying the upstream gradient by the local gradient and summing over fan-outs. This computes $\partial L/\partial w$ for every tracked parameter.

Results are written into each tensor's **.grad** attribute, and crucially they are **accumulated (added)** rather than overwritten. This accumulation is useful for, e.g., summing gradients across micro-batches, but it means that if you don't reset between steps the gradients from previous iterations would pile up and corrupt the update. Hence the standard loop **zeros the gradients** (optimizer.zero_grad() / model.zero_grad()) before each backward(). The conceptual model is identical to a hand-built autograd engine (a Value class storing inputs plus a backward closure per op), just heavily optimized.

descriptive.q: Compare computing gradients by backpropagation versus computing them numerically (finite differences). When is each appropriate?
descriptive.answer:
**Numerical (finite-difference) gradients** approximate $\frac{\partial L}{\partial w}\approx\frac{L(w+\epsilon)-L(w-\epsilon)}{2\epsilon}$ by perturbing each parameter and re-running the forward pass. It is **trivial to implement** and requires no derivation, but it is **catastrophically expensive**: it needs a forward pass for each perturbation, so $O(P)$ forward passes for $P$ parameters — infeasible for networks with millions or billions of weights. It is also subject to numerical error from the choice of $\epsilon$ (too large → truncation error; too small → floating-point cancellation).

**Backpropagation** computes **all** gradients in essentially a **single backward pass** whose cost is comparable to one forward pass, regardless of parameter count, by reusing shared intermediate computations via the chain rule. This efficiency is exactly why it scales to billions of parameters.

**When to use each:** use backprop (autograd) for all real training. Use numerical gradients only as a **gradient check** — a debugging tool to verify that an analytic/custom backward implementation is correct, by comparing the two on a few parameters at small scale. Their agreement (e.g. matching at test points like $x=1.0,\,0.5,\,-2.0$) confirms the backprop derivation is right; their disagreement signals a bug in the local-gradient logic.

topics.title: Loss Functions & Optimization
topics.summary: This session completes the neural-network training loop by answering two questions: what to minimize (loss functions) and how to use gradients (optimizers). It covers MSE vs cross-entropy and why cross-entropy + sigmoid avoids vanishing gradients, the spectrum of batch/stochastic/mini-batch gradient descent with momentum, adaptive methods (AdaGrad, RMSProp, Adam with bias correction), and learning-rate schedules such as warmup and cosine annealing.

notes.heading: The Training Loop: Loss + Optimizer
notes.body:
Backprop gives **gradients**; the loss says *what* to minimize and the optimizer says *how* to use the gradients.

- **Loss function** $L(\hat y, y)$: measures how wrong a prediction is — the 'grading rubric'.
- **Optimizer**: the update rule that turns $\nabla_\theta L$ into a parameter change.
- Generic update: $$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta\, g_t,\quad g_t=\nabla_\theta L(\theta_t)$$
- A complete recipe = **loss + optimizer + LR schedule**. Default starting point: cross-entropy (classification) or MSE (regression), **Adam** with $\eta=0.001$, plus a schedule (warmup + cosine).

notes.heading: MSE — Regression Loss
notes.body:
**Mean Squared Error** squares the error; natural for continuous targets.
$$L_{\text{MSE}}=\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(\hat y_i-y_i)^2$$

- Gradient is **proportional to the error**: for a single term, $\dfrac{\partial}{\partial \hat y}(\hat y-y)^2=2(\hat y-y)$ (the averaged loss adds a $1/N$ factor).
- Corresponds to **maximum likelihood under Gaussian noise**.
- Penalizes large errors quadratically (sensitive to outliers).
- **Problem with sigmoid output**: by the chain rule the gradient w.r.t. the pre-activation picks up $\sigma'(z)$, which **saturates** (see vanishing-gradient note).

notes.heading: Cross-Entropy — Classification Loss
notes.body:
**Cross-entropy** uses a logarithmic penalty — *devastating* for confident wrong answers.

- **Binary CE**: $$L=-\big[y\log\hat y+(1-y)\log(1-\hat y)\big]$$
- **Categorical CE** (one-hot $y$): $$L=-\sum_{k} y_k\log \hat y_k$$
- **Softmax** produces the probability vector for multiclass: $$\hat y_k=\frac{e^{z_k}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}$$
- Log-space intuition: $-\log(0.99)\approx 0.01$ (almost no penalty), $-\log(0.01)\approx 4.6$ (huge penalty). Confidence in the *wrong* class is punished severely.
- Equivalent to **maximum likelihood / minimizing KL divergence** between the true and predicted distributions.

notes.heading: Why CE + Sigmoid Beats MSE + Sigmoid
notes.body:
This is the headline result of the session.

- Sigmoid: $\sigma(z)=\frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$, derivative $\sigma'(z)=\sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z))\le 0.25$, and $\to 0$ at saturation.
- **MSE + sigmoid** gradient w.r.t. pre-activation $z$ (single term): $$\frac{\partial L}{\partial z}=2(\hat y-y)\,\sigma'(z)$$ The $\sigma'(z)$ factor **vanishes** when the neuron is saturated (very wrong but confident) — learning stalls.
- **Cross-entropy + sigmoid** gradient: $$\frac{\partial L}{\partial z}=\hat y-y$$ The $\sigma'(z)$ term **cancels exactly**. The gradient stays proportional to the error regardless of saturation → no vanishing gradient, faster convergence, higher accuracy.

notes.heading: Gradient Descent Variants
notes.body:
How much data per update determines speed vs stability.

- **Batch GD**: full dataset per step — stable, smooth, but slow and memory-heavy.
- **Stochastic GD (SGD)**: one example per step — fast, noisy; noise can help escape shallow minima.
- **Mini-batch GD**: the sweet spot, batch size **32–256**; vectorizes well on GPUs and gives good gradient estimates.
- Update: $\theta\leftarrow\theta-\eta\,\nabla_\theta L_{\text{batch}}$. Smaller batches → noisier gradient, often need a smaller/decaying $\eta$.

notes.heading: Momentum
notes.body:
Adds **velocity** so the optimizer rolls downhill instead of stopping each step.

- Update with momentum coefficient $\beta$ (typically **0.9**), EMA form:
$$v_t=\beta v_{t-1}+(1-\beta)g_t,\qquad \theta_t=\theta_{t-1}-\eta\,v_t$$
(a common alternative is the classical form $v_t=\beta v_{t-1}+g_t$, which differs only by a constant rescaling of $\eta$).
- **Builds velocity** in consistent gradient directions and **cancels oscillations** in directions where the gradient flips sign (e.g. across a narrow ravine).
- It is an **exponential moving average (EMA)** of gradients: with $\beta=0.9$ the effective window is $\approx 1/(1-\beta)=10$ steps.
