descriptive.q: Explain multi-head attention: why use multiple heads instead of one large attention, and how the dimensions work out.
descriptive.answer:
A single attention head produces one weighted-average view of the sequence, biased toward one kind of relationship. **Multi-head attention** runs $h$ heads in parallel, each with its own learned projections $W_i^Q,W_i^K,W_i^V$, so different heads can specialize in different **representation subspaces** — e.g., local syntax, long-range coreference, or semantic similarity. Formally:
$$\text{head}_i=\text{Attention}(QW_i^Q,KW_i^K,VW_i^V),\quad \text{MHA}=\text{Concat}(\text{head}_1,\dots,\text{head}_h)W^O.$$

**Dimensions.** With $d_k=d_v=d_{model}/h$, each head is narrower; concatenating $h$ heads yields $h\cdot d_v=d_{model}$, and $W^O$ mixes them back into a $d_{model}$ output. Because each head is $1/h$ the width, total compute is comparable to one full-width head — you gain diversity essentially for free.

**Why not one big head?** A single softmax average tends to blend everything into one consensus; it cannot simultaneously sharply attend to several distinct positions for different reasons. Splitting into subspaces lets the model represent multiple relationship types in the same layer, which empirically improves expressiveness.

descriptive.q: What is positional encoding, why is it necessary, and what advantage do sinusoidal encodings offer over simply numbering positions?
descriptive.answer:
**Why necessary.** Self-attention treats the input as an unordered set: reorder the tokens and (absent position info) the outputs just reorder identically, so the model literally cannot tell 'dog bites man' from 'man bites dog'. Order must be injected.

**Sinusoidal scheme.** The original Transformer adds fixed sinusoids to embeddings:
$$PE_{(pos,2i)}=\sin\!\big(pos/10000^{2i/d_{model}}\big),\quad PE_{(pos,2i+1)}=\cos\!\big(pos/10000^{2i/d_{model}}\big).$$
Different dimensions oscillate at geometrically spaced frequencies, giving each position a unique multi-scale signature.

**Advantages over a raw index.** A single scalar index grows unbounded and provides no smooth notion of distance. Sinusoids are bounded, and crucially $PE_{pos+k}$ is a fixed linear transform of $PE_{pos}$, so the model can learn to attend by **relative offset**. They also **extrapolate** to sequence lengths never seen in training, whereas learned positional embeddings are undefined beyond the trained range. The encoding is added (not concatenated), letting position and content share the same $d_{model}$ space.

descriptive.q: Explain why the decoder uses masked (causal) self-attention while the encoder does not, and describe the three attention sublayers in an encoder-decoder Transformer.
descriptive.answer:
**Why mask the decoder.** Generation is **autoregressive**: token $t$ is predicted from tokens $<t$ only. During parallel training the decoder sees the whole target sequence at once, so a **causal mask** sets score entries for future positions ($j>i$) to $-\infty$ before softmax, forcing their weights to 0. Without this, the model could 'cheat' by looking ahead and would fail at inference, when future tokens don't exist. The **encoder** processes a fully available input, so it attends bidirectionally with no causal mask (only padding masks).

**Three attention sublayers:**
1. **Encoder self-attention** — $Q,K,V$ from the input; bidirectional, unmasked.
2. **Decoder masked self-attention** — $Q,K,V$ from generated-so-far targets; causal mask prevents future leakage.
3. **Encoder-decoder cross-attention** — queries from the decoder, keys/values from the encoder output, letting each output position attend over the entire source. This is the bridge that conditions generation on the input.

descriptive.q: What is the role of residual connections and layer normalization ('Add & Norm') and the position-wise feed-forward network in each Transformer block?
descriptive.answer:
**Add & Norm.** Every sublayer is wrapped as $\text{LayerNorm}(x+\text{Sublayer}(x))$.
- The **residual** skip provides a direct gradient highway — the same rationale as **ResNet** — so stacking many blocks doesn't cause vanishing gradients, and each sublayer only has to learn a *refinement* of its input.
- **Layer normalization** standardizes activations per token across the feature dimension, stabilizing scale and accelerating/robustifying training. (Pre-norm vs. post-norm placement affects stability of very deep stacks.)

**Feed-forward network.** After attention mixes information *across* positions, a **position-wise FFN** transforms each position *independently and identically*:
$$\text{FFN}(x)=\max(0,xW_1+b_1)W_2+b_2,$$
usually with an expanded inner dimension (e.g. $4\times d_{model}$). It adds per-token nonlinear capacity and feature mixing that attention alone (a weighted average) cannot provide. Together: attention = cross-token communication; FFN = per-token computation; Add & Norm = stable, trainable depth.

descriptive.q: What would change if you (a) doubled the number of heads while keeping $d_{model}$ fixed, and (b) doubled $d_{model}$ while keeping the number of heads fixed?
descriptive.answer:
**(a) Double heads, fix $d_{model}$.** With $d_k=d_{model}/h$, each head shrinks (e.g., 64-dim heads become 32-dim). You get more, finer-grained attention patterns but each head spans a smaller subspace and may capture less per-head information; total attention compute stays roughly the same (the sum of head sizes is unchanged). Too many tiny heads can underfit individual relationships. Note $d_{model}$ should remain divisible by $h$.

**(b) Double $d_{model}$, fix $h$.** Each head doubles in width ($d_k$ doubles), so per-head representational capacity grows. Self-attention compute is $O(n^2 d)$, so it doubles; the FFN (with inner $\sim 4d_{model}$) and all projection matrices grow quadratically in $d_{model}$, substantially increasing parameters and FLOPs/memory. The $\sqrt{d_k}$ scaling automatically adapts to the larger $d_k$. Expect higher capacity and likely better accuracy at materially higher cost and greater overfitting risk on small data.

descriptive.q: Trace the full forward path of an encoder-decoder Transformer for a translation task, and explain how attention complexity scales with input length.
descriptive.answer:
**Forward path.**
1. **Embedding + positional encoding:** source tokens are embedded to $d_{model}$ and summed with positional encodings.
2. **Encoder stack ($N$ blocks):** each block does bidirectional self-attention $\to$ Add & Norm $\to$ position-wise FFN $\to$ Add & Norm, producing contextual source representations.
3. **Decoder stack ($N$ blocks):** each block does *masked* self-attention over generated targets $\to$ Add & Norm $\to$ *cross-attention* (queries from decoder, keys/values from encoder output) $\to$ Add & Norm $\to$ FFN $\to$ Add & Norm.
4. **Output head:** a linear projection to vocabulary size + softmax gives next-token probabilities; training uses cross-entropy with teacher forcing, inference decodes autoregressively (e.g., greedy/beam).

**Complexity scaling.** A self-attention layer forms an $n\times n$ score matrix, so compute is $O(n^2 d)$ and memory $O(n^2)$ in the sequence length $n$. Doubling $n$ roughly quadruples both. This quadratic growth is the central scalability bottleneck for long documents and motivates efficient-attention variants (sparse, low-rank, linear). Encoder-decoder cross-attention scales as $O(n_{src}\,n_{tgt}\,d)$ in the two lengths.

topics.title: BERT & GPT
topics.summary: This session covers BERT and GPT, the two foundational Transformer-derived models that defined modern NLP. It contrasts BERT's bidirectional, encoder-only, masked-language-modeling approach (for understanding tasks) with GPT's causal, decoder-only, next-token-prediction approach (for generation), and ties both to the pre-training plus fine-tuning paradigm. It also explains subword tokenization (BPE/WordPiece), why tokenization breaks LLM arithmetic, sampling temperature, and emergent few-shot in-context learning.

notes.heading: The Pre-training + Fine-tuning Paradigm
notes.body:
- **Pre-training**: train a large model on **massive unlabeled text** with a self-supervised objective (MLM for BERT, next-token for GPT). Learns general **language patterns** once — expensive.
- **Fine-tuning**: start from pre-trained weights and adapt to a specific labeled task with a **small dataset, small learning rate (e.g. $2\times10^{-5}$), few epochs**. Cheap, per task.
- Analogy: medical school (pre-train, once) then residency (fine-tune, per specialty).
- **Why it works**: most of the network already encodes syntax/semantics; only a thin task head + light weight updates are needed. Made training task-specific models **from scratch obsolete**.
- **From scratch** = random init, needs ~$100\times$ more labeled data and overfits on small sets.

notes.heading: BERT — Bidirectional Encoder
notes.body:
- **Encoder-only** Transformer; uses **full (bidirectional) self-attention** — every token attends to all others, left and right.
- Pre-training task: **Masked Language Modeling (MLM)**. Randomly mask ~15% of tokens (the `[MASK]` token) and predict them from **both-side context**.
- Also originally trained with **Next Sentence Prediction (NSP)**.
- Excels at **understanding** tasks: classification, **NER**, question answering, sentence-pair tasks.
- **Cannot generate** text autoregressively: it sees all positions at once, so predicting token 5 would illegally peek at tokens 6+. No causal ordering ⇒ no sequential generation.
- MLM loss is **cross-entropy** over the vocabulary at masked positions, conditioned on the unmasked context: $$\mathcal{L}=-\sum_{i\in\text{masked}}\log p(x_i\mid x_{\setminus\text{masked}})$$

notes.heading: GPT — Causal Decoder
notes.body:
- **Decoder-only** Transformer; uses **causal (masked) self-attention** — token $t$ attends only to positions $\le t$ (left-to-right).
- Pre-training task: **next-token prediction** (autoregressive language modeling). Maximize $$\prod_t p(x_t\mid x_{<t})\quad\Leftrightarrow\quad \mathcal{L}=-\sum_t \log p(x_t\mid x_{<t})$$
- Generates text **one token at a time**, feeding each prediction back in.
- Excels at **generation** (and, at scale, many tasks via prompting).
- The causal mask sets future-position attention logits to $-\infty$ before softmax so they get **zero weight**.
- **Key insight**: the *only* architectural difference between BERT and GPT is the **attention mask** (full vs. causal).

notes.heading: Self-Attention & Its Quadratic Cost
notes.body:
- Scaled dot-product attention: $$\text{Attention}(Q,K,V)=\text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{QK^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V$$
- The $\sqrt{d_k}$ divisor keeps dot products from growing large and pushing softmax into saturated, low-gradient regions.
- $QK^\top$ is an **$n\times n$** matrix for sequence length $n$ ⇒ **$O(n^2)$** time and memory.
- **Doubling $n$ quadruples cost**: $2n\times 2n = 4n^2$ entries. This quadratic scaling is the main bottleneck limiting context length.
- **Attention mask** is added to the logits before softmax: bidirectional = all zeros (allow all); causal = upper triangle $-\infty$ (block future).

notes.heading: Subword Tokenization (BPE / WordPiece)
notes.body:
- Text must become **integer IDs**. Subword tokenizers split rare words into **frequent sub-pieces**, balancing vocabulary size vs. sequence length.
- **BPE (Byte-Pair Encoding)**: start from characters, **greedily merge the most frequent adjacent pair** repeatedly until a target vocab size. Used by GPT.
- **WordPiece**: similar merging but chooses the pair that most increases **corpus likelihood**, not just raw frequency. Used by BERT; marks word-continuations with `##` (e.g. `un` + `##happiness`).
- Benefits: no out-of-vocabulary words (fall back to characters/bytes), compact vocab, shared morphology (`unhappiness` → `un`+`happiness`).
- BERT special tokens: `[CLS]` (sequence summary for classification), `[SEP]` (separator), `[MASK]`, `[PAD]`.

notes.heading: Why LLMs Fail at Arithmetic
notes.body:
- Numbers are tokenized **inconsistently**: `"427"` may become `"42"`+`"7"`; `"15213"` → `["152","13"]`.
- The model sees these as **arbitrary chunk symbols**, not the digits 1-5-2-1-3 with place value.
- Because identical digits land in **different chunks** across numbers, there is no stable per-digit representation to do carries/place arithmetic ⇒ **hallucinated** results.
- Lesson: **the tokenizer determines what the model can even see** — it is the invisible bottleneck. The same characters can yield different token splits depending on surrounding context.
- Remedies: digit-level tokenization, or splitting numbers into single digits, gives a stable per-digit signal.

notes.heading: Sampling & Temperature
notes.body:
- At each step GPT produces logits $z$; sample from the **temperature-scaled softmax** distribution: $$p_i=\frac{e^{z_i/T}}{\sum_j e^{z_j/T}}$$
- **Temperature $T$** rescales logits before softmax:
  - **Low $T$ ($\to 0$)**: distribution **peaked/deterministic**, approaches the argmax — coherent but repetitive/boring.
  - **High $T$ ($>1$)**: distribution **flattened toward uniform** — creative/diverse but more incoherent and error-prone.
  - **$T=1$**: the model's raw distribution.
- Related controls: **greedy/argmax** (deterministic), **top-k** and **top-p (nucleus)** truncate the tail before sampling.
- **Teacher forcing** (training): feed the *true* previous token rather than the model's own prediction, stabilizing training; at inference the model must use its own outputs (exposure bias).

notes.heading: Scaling, Emergence & In-Context Learning
notes.body:
- GPT scaled from **117M (GPT-2 small)** to **175B (GPT-3)** parameters, unlocking abilities **nobody explicitly programmed** (emergent capabilities).
- **In-context / few-shot learning**: GPT-3 can do a task (e.g. translation) given just **2–3 examples in the prompt and NO weight updates**.
- **Zero-shot** = task description only; **one-shot** = 1 example; **few-shot** = a handful — all at inference time.
- Surprising because learning normally requires gradient updates; here it is **pure pattern recognition from the prompt context**.
- Practical model choice: for *understanding* (e.g. classify 1000 emails) fine-tune **BERT**; for open-ended *generation* use **GPT**. With only a few hundred labels, **fine-tune** rather than train from scratch.

quiz.q: What is the single architectural difference that distinguishes BERT from GPT?
options: The number of layers (BERT is deeper)
The attention mask: BERT uses bidirectional (full) attention, GPT uses causal (masked) attention
BERT uses convolutions while GPT uses self-attention
BERT has no feed-forward sublayers
quiz.explanation: Both are Transformers built from the same blocks; the defining difference is that BERT (encoder) allows every token to attend in both directions, while GPT (decoder) masks future positions for left-to-right generation. Depth/FFN/convolutions are not the distinguishing factor.

quiz.q: Why can't BERT generate text autoregressively despite understanding language well?
options: Its vocabulary is too small
It has no softmax output layer
Its bidirectional attention lets every position see all others, so generating token 5 would peek at tokens 6+, violating causal order
It was never trained on enough data
quiz.explanation: Autoregressive generation requires that token t depend only on tokens before it. BERT's full attention sees all positions simultaneously, so there is no valid left-to-right ordering to generate from. Vocabulary size and data are unrelated; BERT does have a softmax.

quiz.q: Doubling the input sequence length increases self-attention memory/compute by roughly what factor?
options: 2x
4x
8x
Unchanged (linear in sequence length)
quiz.explanation: The $QK^\top$ score matrix is $n\times n$. Doubling $n$ gives $2n\times 2n = 4n^2$ entries, so cost scales quadratically — a 4x increase. It is not linear.

quiz.q: GPT-3 translating from 2-3 prompt examples with no gradient updates is called:
options: Transfer fine-tuning
Few-shot (in-context) learning
Self-supervised pre-training
Knowledge distillation
quiz.explanation: Adapting to a task from a few examples placed in the prompt, with no weight changes, is few-shot / in-context learning. Fine-tuning and distillation both modify weights; pre-training is the prior large-scale training stage.

quiz.q: A tokenizer splits '15213' into ['152','13']. Why does this cause arithmetic errors?
options: The tokens exceed the model's context window
The model treats '152' and '13' as arbitrary chunk symbols, not the digits 1-5-2-1-3 with place value, so per-digit arithmetic fails
Numbers are stored as floats and lose precision
The softmax cannot represent integers
quiz.explanation: Inconsistent subword chunking means there is no stable per-digit representation, so the model cannot reliably do carries/place-value arithmetic. It is not a context-window, float-precision, or softmax limitation.

quiz.q: In scaled dot-product attention, what is the purpose of dividing by $\sqrt{d_k}$?
options: To normalize the output to be a probability distribution
To prevent large dot products from pushing softmax into saturated, low-gradient regions
To reduce the number of parameters
To make attention causal
quiz.explanation: For large $d_k$, dot products grow in magnitude and saturate the softmax, yielding tiny gradients. Scaling by $\sqrt{d_k}$ keeps logits at a reasonable scale. Softmax (not the scaling) produces the distribution; the scaling does not affect parameter count or causality.

quiz.q: Which pre-training objective does BERT use?
options: Next-token (autoregressive) prediction
Masked Language Modeling: predict randomly masked tokens from both-side context
Contrastive image-text matching
Reconstruction via an autoencoder bottleneck
quiz.explanation: BERT masks ~15% of tokens and predicts them using bidirectional context (MLM), originally combined with Next Sentence Prediction. Next-token prediction is GPT's objective; the others are unrelated.

quiz.q: You have 500 labeled support emails to classify. Best approach?
options: Train a Transformer classifier from scratch on the 500 emails
Fine-tune a pre-trained BERT model
Use GPT-2 with temperature 1.5 to generate labels
Train a BERT from random initialization with NSP only
quiz.explanation: 500 labels are far too few to train from scratch (severe overfitting). Fine-tuning leverages BERT's existing language knowledge with a small lr/few epochs, typically reaching high accuracy. High-temperature generation is for creative text, not reliable classification; random-init BERT discards the pre-training advantage.

quiz.q: Lowering the sampling temperature $T$ toward 0 makes GPT's output distribution:
options: More uniform and random
More peaked/deterministic, approaching the argmax token
Unchanged, since temperature only affects training
Invalid, because probabilities no longer sum to 1
quiz.explanation: $p_i \propto e^{z_i/T}$: small $T$ sharpens the distribution toward the highest-logit token (more deterministic/coherent, less diverse). High $T$ flattens it. Temperature is an inference-time control and softmax still normalizes to 1.

quiz.q: BPE (Byte-Pair Encoding) builds its vocabulary by:
options: Splitting on whitespace and keeping whole words
Starting from characters and greedily merging the most frequent adjacent pair until a target vocab size
Assigning one token per unique sentence
Randomly grouping characters into fixed-length chunks
quiz.explanation: BPE iteratively merges the most frequent adjacent symbol pair, growing from characters to subwords, which keeps the vocab compact and avoids out-of-vocabulary words. Whitespace-only tokenization and the other options are not how BPE works.

quiz.q: What does 'teacher forcing' refer to when training an autoregressive language model?
options: Forcing the teacher network to match a student via distillation
Feeding the ground-truth previous token (rather than the model's own prediction) as input at each step
Freezing the encoder while training the decoder
Increasing temperature during training to add noise
quiz.explanation: Teacher forcing supplies the true previous token during training, stabilizing and speeding convergence; at inference the model must consume its own outputs (causing exposure bias). It is unrelated to distillation, layer freezing, or temperature.

quiz.q: Which statement about fine-tuning vs. training from scratch is correct?
options: Fine-tuning uses a large learning rate and random initialization
Training from scratch needs roughly 100x less data than fine-tuning
Fine-tuning starts from pre-trained weights, uses a small learning rate and few epochs, and needs far less labeled data
Both require equal amounts of labeled data to reach the same accuracy
quiz.explanation: Fine-tuning reuses pre-trained weights with a small lr and few epochs on a small labeled set; from-scratch uses random init and needs ~100x more data. So fine-tuning is the data-efficient option, the opposite of the other choices.

descriptive.q: Explain the pre-training + fine-tuning paradigm and why it made training task-specific models from scratch obsolete.
descriptive.answer:
**Pre-training** trains a large model on **massive unlabeled text** with a self-supervised objective — Masked Language Modeling for BERT, next-token prediction for GPT. No human labels are needed because the text itself provides the supervision signal (predict a hidden/next token). This one-time, expensive stage teaches the model **general syntax, semantics, world knowledge, and useful representations**.

**Fine-tuning** then takes those pre-trained weights and adapts them to a specific labeled task using a **small dataset, small learning rate (e.g. $2\times10^{-5}$), and few epochs**, usually just by adding a thin task head (e.g. a classification layer on the `[CLS]` token).

It makes from-scratch training obsolete because: (1) the hard, data-hungry part — learning language — is already done; (2) downstream tasks rarely have enough labeled data to learn language *and* the task simultaneously (training from scratch needs ~$100\times$ more data and overfits on small sets); (3) one pre-trained backbone is reused across many tasks, amortizing cost. Analogy: medical school once (pre-train), then a short residency per specialty (fine-tune).

descriptive.q: Compare BERT and GPT across architecture, training objective, attention pattern, and the tasks each is best at. What is the minimal architectural difference?
descriptive.answer:
| Aspect | **BERT** | **GPT** |
|---|---|---|
| Architecture | Encoder-only | Decoder-only |
| Attention | **Bidirectional / full** (each token sees all others) | **Causal / masked** (token $t$ sees only $\le t$) |
| Objective | **Masked Language Modeling** (+ NSP): predict masked tokens from both sides | **Next-token prediction**: $\prod_t p(x_t\mid x_{<t})$ |
| Strength | **Understanding**: classification, NER, QA, sentence-pair | **Generation**: open-ended text, and many tasks via prompting at scale |
| Generation | Cannot generate autoregressively | Generates one token at a time |

**Minimal difference:** the **attention mask**. Both are stacks of the same Transformer blocks (multi-head attention + FFN + residual/LayerNorm). BERT's mask allows all positions (zeros); GPT's mask blocks future positions (upper triangle set to $-\infty$ before softmax). That single change determines whether the model can 'look ahead' — and therefore whether it understands bidirectionally or generates left-to-right.

descriptive.q: Derive why self-attention has $O(n^2)$ memory and compute, and discuss the practical consequence for context length.
descriptive.answer:
Self-attention computes $\text{softmax}(QK^\top/\sqrt{d_k})V$. With sequence length $n$ and head dimension $d_k$, $Q,K\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times d_k}$. The score matrix $S = QK^\top$ has shape $n\times n$ — one similarity per ordered token pair — costing $O(n^2 d_k)$ to compute and $O(n^2)$ to store.

Doubling the input to $2n$ gives a $2n\times 2n$ matrix with $4n^2$ entries: **four times** the memory and compute. So cost grows **quadratically** in $n$.

**Consequence:** doubling context quadruples the attention cost, which is why long-context models are expensive and why much research targets cheaper attention (sparse, linear, FlashAttention-style IO-aware kernels, or windowed/local attention). Note the FFN sublayers scale linearly in $n$, so for long sequences the $n^2$ attention term dominates memory.

descriptive.q: Explain how sampling temperature controls GPT's generation, including the formula and the coherence-vs-creativity tradeoff.
descriptive.answer:
At each step GPT emits logits $z$. Sampling uses a **temperature-scaled softmax**:
$$p_i = \frac{e^{z_i/T}}{\sum_j e^{z_j/T}}.$$

- **Low $T$ (e.g. 0.2)**: dividing by a small number amplifies logit gaps, so the distribution becomes **peaked** and the model almost always picks the top token. Output is **coherent, focused, but repetitive/predictable**; as $T\to0$ it approaches greedy/argmax decoding.
- **High $T$ (e.g. 1.5)**: logit gaps shrink, the distribution **flattens toward uniform**, and low-probability tokens get sampled more often. Output is **diverse/creative but more incoherent** and error-prone.
- **$T=1$**: the model's raw distribution.

The tradeoff: lower $T$ favors reliability and factual coherence; higher $T$ favors novelty and exploration. Complementary truncation methods — **top-k** and **top-p (nucleus)** — restrict sampling to the most probable tokens to limit incoherence while keeping some diversity.

descriptive.q: Why does subword tokenization (BPE/WordPiece) break LLM arithmetic? Tie your answer to what the tokenizer makes the model 'see'.
descriptive.answer:
A model never sees raw characters or digit place-values — it sees **token IDs** chosen by the tokenizer. Subword tokenizers segment text into frequent pieces, and numbers are segmented **inconsistently**: `"427"` might become `"42"`+`"7"`, and `"15213"` → `["152","13"]`. The same digit can land in different chunks across different numbers.

Consequences: (1) there is **no stable per-digit representation**, so the model cannot reliably align digits by place value or perform carries; (2) it operates on **arbitrary chunk symbols** rather than a digit sequence, so two numerically related strings can have unrelated token sequences. Arithmetic on the underlying digits therefore fails and the model **hallucinates** plausible-looking but wrong answers.

The deeper lesson: **the tokenizer determines what the model can even perceive** — it is the invisible bottleneck of the whole NLP pipeline. Remedies include digit-level tokenization or splitting numbers into single digits.

descriptive.q: What is in-context (few-shot) learning, how does it differ from fine-tuning, and why is it surprising?
descriptive.answer:
**In-context learning** is when a model performs a new task by reading a **task description and/or a few worked examples placed directly in the prompt** — and then completing the next instance — with **no gradient updates and no weight changes**. Variants: **zero-shot** (description only), **one-shot** (1 example), **few-shot** (a handful).

**Vs. fine-tuning:** fine-tuning permanently **updates the model's weights** via backprop on labeled data; in-context learning changes nothing about the model — the 'learning' happens transiently within a single forward pass over the prompt.

**Why surprising:** the conventional view is that acquiring a new capability requires training (gradient descent). Yet a sufficiently large model (GPT-3, 175B params) can, e.g., translate from 2-3 prompt examples purely by **pattern-matching the prompt's structure**. This is an **emergent** behavior of scale — it strengthens markedly as models grow from ~117M to 175B parameters and was not explicitly programmed.

descriptive.q: A team must classify 1000 incoming emails into categories and has only ~500 labeled examples. Which model and training strategy should they choose, and why?
descriptive.answer:
This is an **understanding/classification** task, so the natural choice is **BERT** (bidirectional encoder excels at classification/NER/QA), not GPT (whose strength is open-ended generation).

For strategy, **fine-tune a pre-trained BERT** rather than train from scratch: 500 labels are far too few to learn both language and the task from random initialization — it would severely overfit. Fine-tuning starts from weights that already encode language, so a small learning rate (~$2\times10^{-5}$) over a few epochs, plus a small classification head on the `[CLS]` token, typically reaches strong accuracy (often 85%+).

Conceptually: the inputs are tokenized with the BERT tokenizer (padding/truncation to a fixed max length), the objective is cross-entropy over the category logits, and a held-out test set measures accuracy. If categories are simple and labels are extremely scarce, a few-shot prompt to a large GPT model is an alternative — but for a fixed, repeatable, low-cost classifier, fine-tuned BERT is the standard answer.

descriptive.q: Contrast BERT's Masked Language Modeling with GPT's next-token prediction as pre-training objectives, including their loss functions and what representations they encourage.
descriptive.answer:
**MLM (BERT):** randomly mask ~15% of tokens and predict each from **bidirectional context**. The loss is cross-entropy over the vocabulary at masked positions only:
$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{MLM}} = -\sum_{i\in\text{masked}} \log p(x_i \mid x_{\setminus\text{masked}}).$$
Because it conditions on both left and right context, it learns **deeply contextual, bidirectional representations** ideal for understanding — but the objective is not directly usable for left-to-right generation.

**Next-token (GPT):** predict each token from its **left context only**:
$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{LM}} = -\sum_t \log p(x_t \mid x_{<t}).$$
This is a proper autoregressive factorization of the joint probability $\prod_t p(x_t\mid x_{<t})$, so the trained model can **sample text token-by-token**.

**Tradeoffs:** MLM uses every position as a training signal in parallel and yields better bidirectional features, but introduces a pre-train/fine-tune mismatch (the `[MASK]` token never appears at inference) and isn't generative. Next-token prediction is naturally generative and matches inference exactly, but each token only sees the past, giving weaker bidirectional understanding for tasks where future context matters.

topics.title: Hands-on: ANN Regression
topics.summary: This session is a hands-on comparison of multilayer-perceptron (MLP) neural networks against classical ML baselines (Linear/Logistic Regression, Random Forest, SVM) on small tabular datasets for regression (California Housing) and binary classification (Heart Disease). The core lessons are the standard PyTorch training loop, the preprocessing pipeline (StandardScaler), the bias-variance tradeoff and regularization (Dropout, L1/L2 weight decay), evaluation metrics (MSE, R², Accuracy, F1, AUC-ROC), and the professional habit of always establishing simple baselines because deep learning rarely wins on small structured data.

notes.heading: When DL Wins vs Classical ML on Tabular Data
notes.body:
- **Classical ML dominates small tabular data** (fewer than ~10K rows). **Random Forest** and **SVM** routinely match or beat MLPs.
- Tree-based models handle **non-linearity, mixed feature types, and missing values** gracefully and need **no normalization** (splits are scale-invariant).
- **Always establish a classical baseline first** — it is a professional habit and prevents chasing marginal DL gains.
- DL becomes worthwhile when: **data scales to 100K+ rows**, feature **interactions are too complex for axis-aligned tree splits**, or inputs are **unstructured** (images, text, audio).
- Example from session: on California Housing, RF ($R^2=0.82$) vs MLP ($R^2=0.79$) — the small gap **rarely justifies** the added tuning, training time, and reduced interpretability. The MLP is *not* useless, but the simpler model is often preferable.

notes.heading: The Five-Step PyTorch Training Loop
notes.body:
Every loop (and every later session) follows the same skeleton; only **architecture and loss** change:
1. **Preprocess** with `StandardScaler` (zero mean, unit variance).
2. **Define model**: hidden layers + activations.
3. **Choose loss**: `MSELoss` (regression), `BCELoss` (binary), `CrossEntropyLoss` (multi-class).
4. **Optimize** with **Adam**.
5. **Monitor train/val curves** to catch overfitting.

The per-batch core is: clear gradients → forward → compute loss → `backward()` → optimizer `step()`. Gradients **accumulate** in PyTorch (each `backward()` adds to existing `.grad`), so clearing them each step is mandatory. Validation runs under `model.eval()` and `torch.no_grad()`.

notes.heading: Feature Scaling: Why StandardScaler Matters
notes.body:
- Neurons compute **weighted sums** $z = Wx+b$. If feature A spans $0$–$100{,}000$ and feature B spans $0$–$1$, the **gradient w.r.t. A's weights dominates**, biasing/slowing convergence.
- `StandardScaler` maps each feature to $x' = \dfrac{x-\mu}{\sigma}$ (zero mean, unit variance), giving **balanced gradient magnitudes** across inputs.
- Skipping it can cause **slow training, instability, or NaNs**, and makes a single learning rate ill-suited to all features.
- **Fit the scaler on the training set only**, then apply to val/test to avoid **data leakage**. Tree models (RF) do not require scaling because splits depend only on feature thresholds, which are invariant to monotonic rescaling.

notes.heading: Output Activations & Loss Pairing
notes.body:
- **Regression**: linear output (no activation), loss $$\text{MSE}=\tfrac1n\sum_i (y_i-\hat y_i)^2.$$
- **Binary classification**: **sigmoid** on the *final neuron only*, $$\sigma(x)=\frac{1}{1+e^{-x}}\in(0,1),$$ with **BCELoss** $$\mathcal{L}=-\big[y\log\hat y+(1-y)\log(1-\hat y)\big].$$
- **Multi-class**: **softmax** $$\text{softmax}(z)_i=\frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}$$ with categorical cross-entropy.
- **Hidden layers use ReLU**; the squashing activation belongs at the output. Note: `BCEWithLogitsLoss` fuses sigmoid + BCE for numerical stability (pass raw logits, no separate sigmoid).

notes.heading: Bias–Variance Tradeoff
notes.body:
Expected error decomposes as $$\mathbb{E}\big[(y-\hat f(x))^2\big]=\underbrace{(\mathbb{E}[\hat f(x)]-f(x))^2}_{\text{Bias}^2}+\underbrace{\mathbb{E}\big[(\hat f(x)-\mathbb{E}[\hat f(x)])^2\big]}_{\text{Variance}}+\underbrace{\sigma^2}_{\text{irreducible noise}}.$$
- **High bias (underfitting)**: model too simple; **both train and val error high**; fix with **more capacity** (layers/units). *Ex: linear model on non-linear data.*
- **High variance (overfitting)**: memorizes data; **low train, high val error**; fix with **regularization or more data**. *Ex: deep MLP on 300 samples.*
- **Random Forests** cut variance via **bagging** (averaging decorrelated trees), explaining strong out-of-the-box small-data generalization. The **irreducible noise** $\sigma^2$ can never be removed by any model.

notes.heading: Regularization: Dropout, L2, L1
notes.body:
- **Dropout**: randomly zeros a fraction (e.g. 0.3) of activations during training, breaking **neuron co-adaptation**; active only in training, disabled at eval. Often the first tool to try for a large train–val gap.
- **L2 / weight decay (Ridge)**: $$\mathcal{L}_{L2}=\mathcal{L}+\lambda\sum_i w_i^2$$ — **shrinks all weights smoothly** toward zero, differentiable everywhere, set via `weight_decay=λ` in Adam.
- **L1 (Lasso)**: $$\mathcal{L}_{L1}=\mathcal{L}+\lambda\sum_i |w_i|$$ — promotes **sparsity** (drives irrelevant weights exactly to 0 → feature selection), **non-differentiable at 0**.
- **Choose**: prefer **L2 for MLPs**; use **L1** when many features are suspected irrelevant. **Early stopping** and **more data** are also regularizers. A 25-point train/val accuracy gap signals **memorization** → start with Dropout.

notes.heading: Classification Metrics: Accuracy, F1, AUC-ROC
notes.body:
- **Precision** $=\frac{TP}{TP+FP}$, **Recall/TPR** $=\frac{TP}{TP+FN}$, **FPR** $=\frac{FP}{FP+TN}$.
- **F1** $=2\cdot\frac{P\cdot R}{P+R}$ (harmonic mean of precision and recall), robust to imbalance.
- **ROC curve**: TPR vs FPR across all thresholds $\tau$. **AUC** $=P(\text{score}(+)>\text{score}(-))$ — threshold-independent ranking measure.
  - $0.5$ = random (diagonal); $1.0$ = perfect; $\ge 0.90$ = excellent.
- **AUC > accuracy on imbalanced data**: with 95% negatives, an "always-negative" model gets 95% accuracy but **AUC = 0.5**. The 0.5 threshold is just one operating point on the ROC curve.

notes.heading: Capacity, Width/Depth, and Optimizer Choices
notes.body:
- **Width sweep**: validation accuracy rises then **plateaus** beyond a sufficiency width — extra units add params without gains (and may slightly overfit).
- **Depth ablation**: more depth helps until **overfitting dominates** (e.g. a 4-layer MLP on 300 samples degrades).
- **Adam** typically converges **faster than SGD/RMSprop** on tabular data (adaptive per-parameter learning rates with momentum).
- **BatchNorm** ($\hat x=\frac{x-\mu_B}{\sqrt{\sigma_B^2+\epsilon}}$, then learnable scale/shift) speeds convergence and adds mild regularization, but its benefit on small tabular data is modest.
- **Schedulers** (`StepLR`, `ReduceLROnPlateau`) stabilize late training; reduce LR on a val-loss plateau to combat late overfitting.

notes.heading: Debugging Training: Symptoms & Fixes
notes.body:
- **Loss not decreasing** → LR too small or wrong loss fn → raise LR ×10, verify criterion matches task.
- **Loss → NaN/exploding** → LR too high or unscaled inputs → reduce LR, apply `StandardScaler`, add **gradient clipping** (`clip_grad_norm_`).
- **Accuracy stuck at 50%** → class imbalance or label-encoding bug (labels must be 0/1, not 1/2).
- **Train ≫ Val** → overfitting → add Dropout, shrink model, get more data.
- **Val loss decreases then rises** → late overfitting → **early stopping** or LR-on-plateau.
- **Val loss oscillates wildly** while train is smooth → LR too high (overshooting) or batch too small (noisy gradients) → cut LR ×10 / raise batch size.

quiz.q: On the California Housing task, Random Forest reaches $R^2=0.82$ and a tuned MLP reaches $R^2=0.79$. What is the best professional interpretation?
options: The MLP is broken and should be discarded immediately
The small gap rarely justifies the MLP's extra tuning, time, and lost interpretability; prefer the simpler model unless data/complexity grows
MLPs are mathematically incapable of regression and should never be used
The result proves R² is an invalid metric for this dataset
quiz.explanation: A small gap on small tabular data is exactly the expected outcome; the simpler, more interpretable RF is usually preferable. DL pays off at 100K+ samples or with complex interactions. The MLP is not 'broken' or 'incapable' (A/C), and R² is perfectly valid here (D).

quiz.q: Why must `StandardScaler` typically be applied before training an MLP but NOT before a Random Forest?
options: Random Forests internally call StandardScaler automatically
MLPs use weighted sums where large-range features dominate gradients, while tree splits are threshold-based and invariant to monotonic rescaling
Scaling would make tree predictions negative
MLPs cannot accept floating-point inputs without scaling
quiz.explanation: Neurons compute $Wx+b$, so a feature spanning 0–100,000 produces far larger gradients than one spanning 0–1, biasing updates. Trees split on thresholds, which are unaffected by monotonic rescaling. RFs do not call any scaler (A), scaling doesn't force negatives (C), and MLPs accept unscaled floats — they just train poorly (D).

quiz.q: An MLP shows 95% train accuracy but 70% validation accuracy. Which single change addresses this most directly first?
options: Increase the learning rate by 10×
Add more hidden layers to increase capacity
Add Dropout (e.g. rate 0.3) to reduce neuron co-adaptation
Remove StandardScaler from the pipeline
quiz.explanation: A 25-point gap is a classic high-variance/overfitting signature; Dropout directly fights memorization by randomly zeroing activations. Adding capacity (B) worsens overfitting, raising LR (A) doesn't address generalization, and removing scaling (D) hurts training.

quiz.q: Plain `BCELoss` requires inputs in the open interval $(0,1)$. Where should the sigmoid be placed?
options: On every hidden layer as well as the output
On the output neuron only; hidden layers use ReLU
Before the input layer to bound the features
Nowhere — plain BCELoss applies sigmoid internally to raw logits
quiz.explanation: Sigmoid belongs on the final output to produce a probability; hidden layers use ReLU. Putting sigmoid everywhere (A) causes vanishing gradients. Sigmoid on inputs (C) is meaningless here. Option D describes `BCEWithLogitsLoss`, not plain `BCELoss`, which expects already-squashed probabilities.

quiz.q: In the bias-variance decomposition $\mathbb{E}[(y-\hat f)^2]=\text{Bias}^2+\text{Variance}+\sigma^2$, which term cannot be reduced by any model choice?
options: The bias term
The variance term
The irreducible noise $\sigma^2$
All three can be driven to zero with enough data
quiz.explanation: $\sigma^2$ is irreducible noise inherent to the data-generating process and is independent of the model. Bias and variance can be traded against each other, but no amount of data or capacity removes $\sigma^2$, so D is false.

quiz.q: A deep 4-layer MLP trained on only 300 samples shows very low train error but high validation error. This is an instance of:
options: High bias / underfitting
High variance / overfitting
Irreducible noise dominating
Vanishing gradients in a shallow network
quiz.explanation: Low train, high val error is the textbook overfitting (high-variance) signature, worsened by a high-capacity model on tiny data. Underfitting (A) shows high train AND val error. It isn't about irreducible noise (C) or vanishing gradients (D), which concern very deep/recurrent nets.

quiz.q: On a dataset with 95% negatives, a model that always predicts 'negative' achieves 95% accuracy. What is its AUC-ROC?
options: 0.95, matching the accuracy
1.0, because it is never wrong on negatives
0.5, equivalent to random guessing
Undefined because there are no true positives predicted
quiz.explanation: AUC measures ranking ability: $P(\text{score}(+)>\text{score}(-))$. A constant predictor assigns identical scores to all samples, providing no separation → AUC = 0.5. This is exactly why AUC is preferred over accuracy on imbalanced data. Accuracy (A) is misleading here, and AUC is well-defined (D).

quiz.q: Which statement best distinguishes L1 from L2 regularization?
options: L1 shrinks all weights uniformly; L2 drives some weights exactly to zero
L2 promotes sparsity; L1 keeps every feature active
L1 promotes sparse (feature-selecting) solutions and is non-differentiable at 0; L2 shrinks weights smoothly and keeps all active
Both produce identical weight distributions but L2 is faster
quiz.explanation: L1 ($\sum|w_i|$) yields sparse solutions and selects features but is non-smooth at 0; L2 ($\sum w_i^2$) shrinks all weights smoothly and keeps them active. Options A and B reverse the two; D is false since their effects differ substantially.

quiz.q: Training loss decreases smoothly but validation loss oscillates wildly. Which pair of causes is most plausible?
options: Learning rate too high and/or batch size too small
Learning rate too low and batch size too large
Missing output activation and wrong dataset
Too much Dropout and too little data
quiz.explanation: A high LR makes updates overshoot the minimum (oscillation), and tiny batches give noisy gradient estimates → unstable val loss. Diagnose by cutting LR ×10 or raising batch size. A low LR + large batch (B) gives smooth-but-slow behavior, not oscillation.

quiz.q: Why do Random Forests generalize well on small tabular datasets with little tuning?
options: They use gradient descent with adaptive learning rates
Bagging averages many decorrelated trees, reducing variance
They apply StandardScaler internally to every feature
Each tree is regularized with L2 weight decay
quiz.explanation: Bagging trains trees on bootstrap samples and averages them; averaging decorrelated predictors reduces variance without inflating bias much, yielding robust small-data performance. RFs don't use gradient descent (A), don't scale features (C), and have no weight-decay penalty (D).

quiz.q: In a width sweep over hidden sizes [8, 16, ..., 512] for a single-layer MLP, what is the expected trend in validation accuracy?
options: Strictly increasing with width without limit
Rises then plateaus once width exceeds a sufficiency threshold
Strictly decreasing because wider models always overfit
Random with no relationship to width
quiz.explanation: Once the network has enough capacity to fit the signal, extra width adds parameters with diminishing returns — accuracy plateaus (and may slightly drop from overfitting). It does not increase without bound (A) or strictly decrease (C), and the relationship is systematic, not random (D).

quiz.q: Conceptually, within one training iteration the optimizer must clear gradients, run a forward pass, compute the loss, backpropagate, then update weights. Why must gradients be cleared at the start of each iteration in PyTorch?
options: Because PyTorch accumulates (adds) new gradients onto existing `.grad` values, so without clearing, stale gradients from prior batches pile up and corrupt the update
Because clearing gradients is what triggers the forward pass to execute
Because the loss cannot be computed while gradients are non-zero
Because weights are only updated when `.grad` is exactly zero
quiz.explanation: By default `backward()` adds gradients into existing `.grad` buffers rather than overwriting them, so each iteration must reset them or updates use an inflated, mis-directed gradient sum. Clearing does not trigger forward (B), loss is independent of stored gradients (C), and updates use the gradient, not require it to be zero (D).

descriptive.q: Explain why classical ML models (Random Forest, SVM) frequently outperform MLPs on small structured/tabular datasets, and state concretely the conditions under which deep learning becomes the better choice.
descriptive.answer:
On small tabular data (fewer than ~10K rows), MLPs face a **high-variance problem**: they have many parameters and little data, so they tend to memorize rather than generalize, and they require careful tuning of architecture, learning rate, and regularization.

Classical models exploit structure that helps on tabular data:
- **Random Forests** reduce variance via **bagging** — averaging many decorrelated trees trained on bootstrap samples — so they generalize well *out of the box* with minimal tuning. Trees natively handle **non-linearity, mixed feature types, and missing values**, and are **scale-invariant** (no normalization needed).
- **SVMs** find maximum-margin separators that work well when sample counts are small and margins are clear.

Deep learning wins when:
- **Scale grows** (100K+ rows) so the data can support many parameters.
- **Feature interactions are too complex** for axis-aligned tree splits.
- Data is **unstructured** (images, text, audio) where learned hierarchical representations dominate — e.g. the session's MNIST challenge, where a (C)NN/MLP trained on 60K images beats RF/SVM.

The professional habit: **always establish a simple baseline first**, then only invest in DL when it clears the bar by a margin that justifies its cost in tuning, compute, and interpretability.

descriptive.q: Derive and interpret the bias-variance decomposition of expected squared error, and connect each term to underfitting, overfitting, and the behavior of Random Forests.
descriptive.answer:
For a true function $f(x)$ with noisy observations $y=f(x)+\varepsilon$, $\mathbb{E}[\varepsilon]=0$, $\text{Var}(\varepsilon)=\sigma^2$, and an estimator $\hat f(x)$, the expected squared error at $x$ decomposes as:
$$\mathbb{E}\big[(y-\hat f(x))^2\big]=\underbrace{\big(\mathbb{E}[\hat f(x)]-f(x)\big)^2}_{\text{Bias}^2}+\underbrace{\mathbb{E}\big[(\hat f(x)-\mathbb{E}[\hat f(x)])^2\big]}_{\text{Variance}}+\underbrace{\sigma^2}_{\text{irreducible}}.$$
The cross terms vanish because $\varepsilon$ is independent of $\hat f$ and has mean zero.

Interpretation:
- **Bias** measures systematic error from an overly simple model → **underfitting**, where *both train and val error are high* (e.g. a linear model on non-linear data). Fix with **more capacity**.
- **Variance** measures sensitivity to the particular training sample → **overfitting**, where *train error is low but val error is high* (e.g. a deep MLP on 300 samples). Fix with **regularization or more data**.
- **$\sigma^2$** is irreducible noise — no model can remove it.

**Random Forests** specifically attack the **variance** term: averaging $T$ decorrelated trees reduces variance roughly by a factor related to $1/T$ (modulated by the inter-tree correlation $\rho$, since variance of the average $\to \rho\,\sigma_{\text{tree}}^2$ as $T\to\infty$) without inflating bias, which is why they generalize well on small datasets with little tuning.

descriptive.q: Compare L1 and L2 regularization mathematically and practically. When would you choose each for an MLP?
descriptive.answer:
Both add a penalty to the training loss $\mathcal{L}$:
$$\mathcal{L}_{L2}=\mathcal{L}+\lambda\sum_i w_i^2 \qquad \mathcal{L}_{L1}=\mathcal{L}+\lambda\sum_i |w_i|.$$

**L2 (Ridge / weight decay)**: the gradient of the penalty is $2\lambda w_i$, proportional to the weight, so it **shrinks all weights smoothly** toward (but not exactly to) zero. It is differentiable everywhere and trivially implemented as `weight_decay=λ` in Adam/SGD. It keeps all features active and yields stable, well-conditioned solutions.

**L1 (Lasso)**: the (sub)gradient is $\lambda\,\text{sign}(w_i)$, a constant-magnitude pull toward zero regardless of weight size, which **drives irrelevant weights exactly to zero**, producing **sparse, feature-selecting** solutions. It is **non-differentiable at $w=0$** (needs subgradients/proximal methods) and typically requires a manual penalty term in PyTorch.

**Choosing**:
- Prefer **L2 for MLPs** — smooth gradients, easy implementation, good default for controlling weight magnitude and the generalization gap.
- Use **L1** when you suspect **many irrelevant features** and want automatic selection / a sparse model (common in linear models via Lasso).
- They can be combined (Elastic Net). Other regularizers — Dropout, early stopping, more data — often complement weight penalties.

descriptive.q: Explain the ROC curve and AUC. Why is AUC preferred over accuracy on imbalanced datasets, and what does the 0.5-threshold operating point represent?
descriptive.answer:
The **ROC curve** plots **True Positive Rate** $\text{TPR}=\frac{TP}{TP+FN}$ against **False Positive Rate** $\text{FPR}=\frac{FP}{FP+TN}$ as the decision threshold $\tau$ sweeps from 1 to 0. Each $\tau$ gives one $(\text{FPR}, \text{TPR})$ point; lowering $\tau$ classifies more samples positive, raising both rates.

**AUC** (area under the curve) has a clean probabilistic meaning:
$$\text{AUC}=P\big(\text{score}(\text{positive})>\text{score}(\text{negative})\big),$$
the probability the model ranks a random positive above a random negative. $\text{AUC}=0.5$ is random (diagonal), $1.0$ is perfect, and $\ge 0.90$ is excellent in practice.

**Why AUC beats accuracy on imbalance**: with 95% negatives, a degenerate "always-negative" classifier scores **95% accuracy** yet has **AUC = 0.5** because it cannot rank positives above negatives. AUC is **threshold-independent** and evaluates ranking quality across all operating points, so it is not fooled by a dominant class.

The **0.5-threshold operating point** is just *one specific point* on the ROC curve — the default cutoff for converting probabilities to labels. AUC integrates over *all* thresholds, so it summarizes performance independent of any particular cutoff. In deployment you may pick a different $\tau$ to trade precision against recall depending on the cost of false positives vs false negatives.

descriptive.q: Walk through the five essential steps of the PyTorch MLP training loop and explain why each step is necessary, including what goes wrong if gradients are not cleared each iteration.
descriptive.answer:
The canonical loop:
1. **Preprocess with `StandardScaler`** — maps each feature to zero mean, unit variance so gradients are balanced; fit on train only to avoid leakage.
2. **Define the model** — hidden layers with **ReLU** activations provide the non-linear capacity; the output layer matches the task (linear for regression, single sigmoid neuron for binary).
3. **Choose the loss** — `MSELoss` for regression, `BCELoss`/`BCEWithLogitsLoss` for binary, `CrossEntropyLoss` for multi-class. The loss must match the output activation.
4. **Optimize with Adam** — adaptive per-parameter learning rates with momentum, robust and fast on tabular data.
5. **Monitor train/val curves** — to detect overfitting (train error ≪ val error) and stop early.

Per batch the mechanics are: `optimizer.zero_grad()` → `y_pred = model(X)` → `loss = criterion(y_pred, y)` → `loss.backward()` → `optimizer.step()`.

**If gradients are not cleared (`zero_grad()` omitted)**: PyTorch **accumulates** gradients by adding new `.grad` values onto existing ones at each `backward()` call. Without clearing, gradients from previous batches **pile up**, so each update uses a stale, inflated gradient sum. This causes effectively **too-large, mis-directed steps**, unstable or diverging training, and incorrect optimization. (Deliberate accumulation is sometimes used to simulate larger batches, but then you clear only every N steps.) Validation must run under `model.eval()` (disables Dropout/uses BN running stats) and `torch.no_grad()` (skips graph building) for correct, efficient evaluation.

descriptive.q: What happens if you skip feature standardization before training an MLP on data where features have wildly different scales? Contrast this with a tree-based model.
descriptive.answer:
**For the MLP**: each neuron computes $z=\sum_i w_i x_i + b$. If feature A ranges 0–100,000 and feature B ranges 0–1, then for comparable weights the contribution and the **gradient magnitude for A's weights vastly exceed B's**. Consequences:
- The optimizer effectively chases the large-scale feature, **biasing and slowing convergence** along B's directions.
- A single learning rate cannot suit both scales — too large for A causes oscillation/overshoot; too small for B stalls learning.
- Extreme magnitudes can push activations into saturated/overflow regions, risking **exploding loss or NaNs**.

Standardization $x'=\frac{x-\mu}{\sigma}$ equalizes feature scales so gradients are balanced and a single LR works well. (Fit on train, apply to val/test to prevent leakage.)

**For a tree-based model (Random Forest)**: splits are chosen by thresholds on individual features (e.g. $x_A \le 5000$). Any **monotonic rescaling leaves the ordering and thus the best split unchanged**, so trees are **scale-invariant** and need no standardization. This scale-robustness — plus native handling of mixed types and missing values — is a major reason trees are convenient and competitive on raw tabular data.

descriptive.q: Describe Dropout: how it works during training versus inference, why it reduces overfitting, and how it relates to ensembling. When might Dropout hurt?
descriptive.answer:
**Mechanism (training)**: Dropout randomly sets a fraction $p$ of a layer's activations to zero **each forward pass** (e.g. $p=0.3$), independently per unit per example. To keep expected activations consistent, surviving units are typically scaled by $\frac{1}{1-p}$ ('inverted dropout').

**Inference**: Dropout is **turned off** — all units are active and no scaling is applied (already handled by inverted dropout during training). This is why `model.eval()` matters: it disables Dropout so predictions are deterministic and use the full network.

**Why it reduces overfitting**: by randomly removing units, no neuron can rely on the presence of specific others, which **breaks co-adaptation** and forces redundant, robust features. A 25-point train/val gap is a strong overfitting signal that Dropout directly targets.

**Ensembling view**: each training step samples a different 'thinned' sub-network; over training you implicitly train an **exponential ensemble of sub-networks sharing weights**, and inference with the full net approximates averaging that ensemble — a variance-reduction effect.

**When it hurts**: if the model is **underfitting** (high bias), Dropout removes needed capacity and worsens both train and val error. Very high $p$ or Dropout on tiny networks/datasets can stall learning. It also slows convergence (noisier gradients). On small tabular data, modest $p$ or pairing with L2 is usually better than aggressive Dropout.

descriptive.q: Compare the design choices for the regression head (California Housing) versus the binary classification head (Heart Disease) of an MLP: output activation, loss function, and evaluation metrics, and justify each.
descriptive.answer:
**Regression (California Housing — predict a continuous house value):**
- **Output activation**: none (linear). The target is an unbounded real number, so a single linear output neuron is appropriate; squashing it would clip the range.
- **Loss**: **MSE** $\frac1n\sum (y-\hat y)^2$ — penalizes large errors quadratically and yields smooth gradients; corresponds to a Gaussian-noise assumption.
- **Metrics**: **MSE/RMSE** (error in target units) and **$R^2$** (fraction of variance explained, $1-\frac{\sum(y-\hat y)^2}{\sum(y-\bar y)^2}$), compared against Linear Regression and Random Forest baselines.

**Binary classification (Heart Disease — diagnose yes/no):**
- **Output activation**: **sigmoid** $\sigma(x)=\frac{1}{1+e^{-x}}$ on the *single output neuron only*, producing a probability in $(0,1)$; hidden layers stay ReLU.
- **Loss**: **BCELoss** $-[y\log\hat y+(1-y)\log(1-\hat y)]$, which expects probabilities and is the correct log-likelihood for Bernoulli targets (or `BCEWithLogitsLoss` for numerical stability from raw logits).
- **Metrics**: **Accuracy, F1, and AUC-ROC**; AUC is emphasized because it is threshold-independent and robust to class imbalance, with the ROC curve and confusion matrix plotted versus Logistic Regression and SVM.

The contrast highlights that the **same training-loop skeleton** is reused; only the **output activation, loss, and metrics** change to match the target type — a pattern that carries through every later session (CrossEntropyLoss for multi-class/sequence tasks).

topics.title: Hands-on: Neural Style Transfer
topics.summary: Neural Style Transfer (Gatys et al., 2016) generates an image that fuses the content of a photo with the style of a painting by running gradient descent on the pixels of an image while a pretrained, frozen VGG19 acts as a fixed feature extractor. Content is matched at a deep layer (conv4_2) via feature-map differences, while style is matched across several shallow-to-deep layers via Gram matrices that capture spatially-invariant feature correlations, with a total-variation term added for smoothness. This session focuses on the underlying concepts: feature hierarchies, content vs. style losses, Gram matrices, the composite objective, and why LBFGS image optimization is used.

notes.heading: The Big Idea: Optimize the Image, Not the Weights
notes.body:
Neural Style Transfer (**NST**) reframes generation as **optimization in pixel space**. A pretrained classification network (**VGG19**) is used purely as a **fixed feature extractor** — its weights are **frozen**.

- The **variable being optimized is the generated image** $x$ (its pixel values), not any network parameter.
- $x$ is typically **initialized as a copy of the content image**, then iteratively nudged so its deep features match the content photo and its feature correlations match the style painting.
- Gradients flow *through* the frozen network back to the **input pixels**: $x \leftarrow x - \eta\, \partial L/\partial x$.
- Modifying VGG weights would destroy the pretrained representations that encode content and style, so they are left untouched.

notes.heading: Feature Hierarchies in VGG19
notes.body:
CNNs learn a **hierarchy of representations**; NST exploits this directly.

- **Shallow layers** (conv1_1, conv2_1) — small receptive fields ($3\times3$ to $\sim7\times7$): edges, color gradients, **local textures / brush strokes** → used for **style**.
- **Middle layers** (conv3_1, conv4_1) — receptive fields $\sim15\times15$ to $31\times31$: textures, simple shapes, **object parts** → also style (higher-level).
- **Deep layers** (conv4_2, conv5_1) — large receptive fields ($31\times31$, $63\times63$): **semantic objects and scene structure** → conv4_2 used for **content**.

**Why conv4_2 for content?** Deep enough to capture semantic layout, but not so deep that fine spatial detail is lost. **Why shallow layers for style?** Style is defined by local patterns (strokes, color, texture), which small-receptive-field filters capture. Only the **convolutional blocks are used**; the fully-connected (fc6–fc8) layers are discarded.

notes.heading: Content Loss
notes.body:
Content is measured by **directly comparing feature maps** at a chosen content layer $\ell$ (conv4_2). Let $F^\ell[x]$ and $P^\ell[p]$ be the feature activations of the generated image $x$ and content photo $p$.

$$L_{\text{content}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\sum_{i,j}\big(F^{\ell}_{ij}[x] - P^{\ell}_{ij}[p]\big)^2$$

- This is a **per-position, per-channel squared difference** — it **preserves spatial arrangement**, so the *where* of objects is retained.
- Using a deep layer means we match **semantic content** ("sky here, mountain there") rather than exact RGB pixels, allowing texture to change while structure stays.

notes.heading: Gram Matrix: Style as Feature Correlations
notes.body:
For a feature map $F^{\ell}\in\mathbb{R}^{C_\ell\times N_\ell}$ ($C_\ell$ channels, $N_\ell = H_\ell W_\ell$ spatial positions), the **normalized Gram matrix** is:

$$G^{\ell}_{ij} = \frac{1}{C_\ell N_\ell}\sum_{k=1}^{N_\ell} F^{\ell}_{ik}\,F^{\ell}_{jk}$$

- It is a **self-product** $G = \tfrac{1}{C N}FF^{\top}$ measuring **pairwise co-activation** of channels $i$ and $j$ — *which textures tend to occur together*.
- The **sum over spatial positions $k$ collapses the spatial dimension** → style becomes **location/translation-invariant** (shifting the image leaves $G$ nearly unchanged).
- Raw feature maps keep *where* a feature is; the Gram matrix keeps only *how features correlate*, discarding position.
- The matrix is **symmetric** ($G_{ij}=G_{ji}$) and **positive semidefinite**, since $G\propto FF^{\top}$.
- **Normalization by $C_\ell N_\ell$ is critical**: without it, Gram entries (and their gradients) grow with the number of channels/positions, making per-layer style weights depend on the layer's resolution.

notes.heading: Style Loss
notes.body:
Style loss compares Gram matrices of the **style image $a$** and **generated image $x$**, summed over several style layers $\ell$.

$$L^{\ell}_{\text{style}} = \tfrac{1}{4}\big\lVert G^{\ell}[x] - G^{\ell}[a]\big\rVert_F^2, \qquad L_{\text{style}} = \sum_{\ell} w_\ell\, L^{\ell}_{\text{style}}$$

- $\lVert\cdot\rVert_F$ is the **Frobenius norm**, so $\lVert M\rVert_F^2=\sum_{i,j}M_{ij}^2$ (sum of squared element differences).
- Using **multiple layers (1–5)** yields **multi-scale** style: shallow layers give fine-grain texture, deeper layers add larger structural motifs. More layers ⇒ richer style.
- Because Gram matrices are spatially invariant, style loss does **not** force the painting's objects into specific locations — only its **texture statistics**.

notes.heading: Total Composite Objective + Total Variation
notes.body:
The full objective is a weighted sum of three terms, minimized over the pixels of $x$:

$$L = \alpha\,L_{\text{content}} + \beta\,L_{\text{style}} + \gamma\,L_{\text{TV}}$$

- **$\alpha$ (content weight, ~1–100):** higher ⇒ output keeps the photo's structure.
- **$\beta$ (style weight, ~$10^4$–$10^6$):** higher ⇒ stronger stylization; too high ⇒ content disappears.
- The **ratio $\alpha/\beta$** controls the content–style trade-off: a smaller $\alpha/\beta$ means more stylization.

**Total Variation (TV) loss** ($\gamma\sim10^{-4}$–$10^{-2}$) enforces **smoothness**:

$$L_{\text{TV}} = \sum_{i,j}\Big(|I_{i,j+1}-I_{i,j}|^2 + |I_{i+1,j}-I_{i,j}|^2\Big)$$

Without it, content and style gradients adjust adjacent pixels independently, producing **high-frequency noise / checkerboard artifacts**. TV penalizes large neighbor-to-neighbor differences, giving locally smooth output.

notes.heading: LBFGS vs Adam for Image Optimization
notes.body:
**LBFGS** is the standard optimizer for classic NST; **Adam** is the general-purpose workhorse for training network weights.

| Aspect | LBFGS (quasi-Newton) | Adam (first-order) |
|---|---|---|
| Order | Approximates **2nd-order curvature** | Uses only **gradients** (with moment estimates) |
| Batching | **Full-batch** per step (whole image) | Supports **mini-batches** |
| Best for | **Smooth, low-dim** problems (single image) | Large datasets / many weights |
| Iterations | **~300** sufficient | ~500–1000 |

- The "dataset" in NST is a **single image** with a **smooth** objective, so LBFGS's curvature exploitation converges fast and no mini-batching is needed.
- For training NN **weights** on large data, Adam wins (robust, scalable, mini-batch friendly). If LBFGS diverges (LR too high), reducing LR or switching to Adam is the fix.

notes.heading: Practical Behavior, Tuning & Variants
notes.body:
**Symptom → fix (cause):**
- Output **noisy/grainy** → increase $\gamma$ (TV too low).
- **Content structure lost** → reduce $\beta$ (style too high) or raise $\alpha$.
- **Unnatural colors** → use **color-preserving NST**: transfer only **luminance** (apply style to the **L channel in LAB**, keep original A/B).
- **Slow convergence** → initialize from the **content image**, not noise.

**Conceptual variants:**
- **Texture synthesis:** set $\alpha=0$, optimize style only from noise → pure texture (no content).
- **Multi-style blending:** $L_{\text{style}} = w_1 L_{\text{style}_1} + w_2 L_{\text{style}_2}$.
- **Video NST:** per-frame NST flickers; add a **temporal consistency loss** $L_{\text{temp}}=\lVert I_t - \text{warp}(I_{t-1},\phi_{t-1\to t})\rVert^2$ using optical flow $\phi$.
- **Fast / feed-forward NST** (Johnson et al.): train a network with **perceptual loss** for ~1000× faster, single-pass stylization. **AdaIN** (Huang & Belongie) aligns feature statistics (mean/variance) for arbitrary real-time style.

quiz.q: In classic Neural Style Transfer, what is the variable being updated by gradient descent?
options: The convolutional weights of VGG19
The pixel values of the generated image
The Gram matrices of the style image
The biases of VGG19's fully-connected layers
quiz.explanation: VGG19 is frozen and used only as a feature extractor; gradients flow back to the input image and update its pixels. The network weights and the fixed target Gram matrices are not optimized; the fc layers aren't even used.

quiz.q: Why is the Gram matrix a good descriptor of style?
options: It records the exact spatial location of each texture
It collapses spatial position and captures pairwise channel co-activations, making it translation-invariant
It measures the semantic class of objects in the image
It preserves the high-resolution pixel intensities of the painting
quiz.explanation: The Gram matrix sums products of activations over all spatial positions, discarding location while encoding which feature channels co-activate. This makes style a spatially-invariant statistic; it does not store location or semantics.

quiz.q: The content loss is computed by comparing raw feature maps (not Gram matrices) at a deep layer. What does this design preserve?
options: The exact RGB color histogram of the photo
The spatial arrangement / semantic structure of the content
The texture correlations of the style image
The translation invariance of style statistics
quiz.explanation: Content loss is a per-position squared difference of feature maps, so it keeps the spatial layout of objects. Gram-based losses would discard that location information; content loss specifically retains it.

quiz.q: You want the output to look MORE like the content photo and less like the painting. What should you do?
options: Increase the style weight $\beta$
Increase the content weight $\alpha$
Increase the TV weight $\gamma$ only
Remove the content layer from the network
quiz.explanation: In $L=\alpha L_{content}+\beta L_{style}+\gamma L_{TV}$, a larger $\alpha$ pulls optimization toward preserving content features. Increasing $\beta$ does the opposite; $\gamma$ only controls smoothness.

quiz.q: Why are shallow VGG layers used for style and deep layers for content?
options: Shallow layers are faster to compute, deep layers are slower
Shallow layers have small receptive fields capturing local texture/strokes (style); deep layers have large receptive fields capturing semantic structure (content)
Shallow layers have more channels, so they encode more style
Deep layers are translation-invariant, which is what style requires
quiz.explanation: Receptive-field size drives the choice: small fields detect local patterns that define artistic style; large fields encode whole objects/scene structure that define content. Channel count and speed are not the reasons, and translation invariance in NST comes from the Gram operation, not from depth.

quiz.q: What artifact does the total-variation (TV) loss prevent, and how?
options: Color washout, by matching color histograms
High-frequency noise / checkerboard artifacts, by penalizing large differences between adjacent pixels
Loss of semantic content, by increasing the content weight
Slow convergence, by adding curvature information
quiz.explanation: TV loss $\sum(|I_{i,j+1}-I_{i,j}|^2+|I_{i+1,j}-I_{i,j}|^2)$ penalizes abrupt neighbor differences, enforcing local smoothness and removing grainy high-frequency noise. It does not affect color matching, content weighting, or optimizer curvature.

quiz.q: Why is LBFGS typically preferred over Adam for classic single-image NST?
options: LBFGS supports mini-batches, which speeds up large datasets
The objective is smooth and the 'dataset' is one image, so LBFGS's curvature use converges in fewer (~300) iterations
LBFGS avoids computing gradients entirely
Adam cannot optimize input pixels, only weights
quiz.explanation: NST optimizes a single image over a smooth objective, ideal for the quasi-Newton, full-batch LBFGS, which exploits curvature and needs ~300 iterations. Adam still works on pixels but typically needs more iterations; LBFGS is full-batch (not mini-batch) and does use gradients.

quiz.q: The normalized Gram matrix divides by $C_\ell N_\ell$. What problem does this normalization solve?
options: It makes the Gram matrix symmetric
It prevents gradient magnitudes from scaling with layer size, so per-layer style weights stay layer-independent
It converts feature correlations into probabilities
It removes the need for a content loss term
quiz.explanation: Without normalization, Gram entries (and their gradients) grow with the number of channels/positions, making the effective style weight depend on layer resolution. Dividing by $C_\ell N_\ell$ keeps contributions comparable across layers. Symmetry comes from $FF^\top$ regardless of normalization.

quiz.q: If you set $\alpha = 0$ (no content loss), initialize from Gaussian noise, and optimize only style loss, what do you get?
options: An exact copy of the content photo
Pure texture synthesis from the style image's statistics, with no specific content
A blurry average of content and style
Nothing — optimization cannot proceed without a content term
quiz.explanation: With only style loss from noise, the optimizer reproduces the style image's texture statistics without any content anchor, yielding neural texture synthesis. The content photo plays no role, and optimization proceeds fine using only style gradients.

quiz.q: Shifting (rolling) the style image by 50 pixels and recomputing its Gram matrices should produce what result?
options: A completely different Gram matrix, because location changed
A nearly identical Gram matrix, because style statistics are spatially invariant
A Gram matrix scaled by 50
A non-symmetric Gram matrix
quiz.explanation: The Gram matrix sums over all spatial positions, so a spatial shift barely changes the channel co-activation statistics, confirming style's translation invariance. It is not scaled by the shift and remains symmetric.

quiz.q: To stylize a video frame-by-frame without distracting flicker, what is added to the objective?
options: A larger content weight on every frame
A temporal consistency loss using optical flow to warp the previous frame, $\lVert I_t-\text{warp}(I_{t-1},\phi)\rVert^2$
A second Gram matrix for motion
A higher TV weight only
quiz.explanation: Independent per-frame NST flickers because each frame is optimized separately. A temporal consistency loss penalizes deviation from the optical-flow-warped previous frame, enforcing coherence across time. TV smooths within a frame, not across frames.

quiz.q: Compared with classic optimization-based NST, what is the key idea of fast/feed-forward style transfer (Johnson et al., 2016)?
options: It optimizes the input pixels for 10,000 iterations per image
It trains a feed-forward network with a perceptual (VGG-feature) loss so stylization is a single fast forward pass
It replaces Gram matrices with pixel-wise L1 loss
It removes VGG entirely from both training and inference
quiz.explanation: Fast NST trains a generator network once using a perceptual loss defined on a fixed VGG, so at test time stylization is one forward pass (~1000x faster). VGG is still used to define the loss during training; it is not per-image pixel optimization.

descriptive.q: Explain why Neural Style Transfer optimizes the generated image's pixels rather than the weights of VGG19, and describe how gradients flow during this process.
descriptive.answer:
VGG19 is used as a **fixed feature extractor**: its weights were trained on ImageNet to encode a rich hierarchy of visual features (edges → textures → object parts → semantics). These pretrained representations are exactly what let us *read off* content (deep feature maps) and style (Gram matrices). If we modified the weights, we would corrupt those representations and lose the very signal NST depends on.

Instead, the **generated image $x$ is the optimization variable**. We define a scalar loss $L(x)=\alpha L_{\text{content}}+\beta L_{\text{style}}+\gamma L_{\text{TV}}$, then compute $\partial L/\partial x$ by backpropagating *through* the frozen network all the way to the input. The update is a gradient step on pixels, e.g. $x \leftarrow x - \eta\,\partial L/\partial x$ (LBFGS in practice).

- $x$ is usually **initialized as a copy of the content image**, which gives fast convergence.
- The chain rule still applies through every conv layer, but the weights are treated as constants — only the input gets updated.
- Conceptually this is "training the image, not the network."

descriptive.q: Compare content loss and style loss. Why does one use raw feature maps while the other uses Gram matrices, and what does each preserve or discard?
descriptive.answer:
**Content loss** compares raw feature activations at a deep layer (conv4_2): $L_{\text{content}}=\tfrac12\sum_{i,j}(F_{ij}[x]-P_{ij}[p])^2$. Because it is a **per-position, per-channel** difference, it *preserves spatial arrangement* — object A stays where it is. Using a deep layer means we match **semantic structure**, not exact RGB pixels, so texture is free to change.

**Style loss** compares **Gram matrices**: $L^{\ell}_{\text{style}}=\tfrac14\lVert G^{\ell}[x]-G^{\ell}[a]\rVert_F^2$ where $G_{ij}=\tfrac{1}{C N}\sum_k F_{ik}F_{jk}$. The sum over spatial positions $k$ **discards location** and keeps only *how channels co-activate* — i.e., texture statistics. This makes style **translation-invariant**.

- The contrast is deliberate: content needs *where* (spatial), so we keep feature maps; style needs *what textures co-occur* regardless of *where*, so we collapse space via the Gram matrix.
- Style is typically summed over **multiple layers** (multi-scale), while content uses a **single** deep layer.

descriptive.q: Derive/justify the Gram matrix as a style descriptor. Show its translation invariance and explain why the $1/(C_\ell N_\ell)$ normalization matters.
descriptive.answer:
Let $F^{\ell}\in\mathbb{R}^{C_\ell\times N_\ell}$ flatten the $H\times W$ spatial grid into $N_\ell=HW$ columns. The Gram matrix is the self-product $G^{\ell}=\tfrac{1}{C_\ell N_\ell}F^{\ell}(F^{\ell})^{\top}$, with entries
$$G^{\ell}_{ij}=\frac{1}{C_\ell N_\ell}\sum_{k=1}^{N_\ell}F^{\ell}_{ik}F^{\ell}_{jk}.$$
Each entry is a (normalized) **inner product between two channels** over all spatial positions — a measure of how strongly features $i$ and $j$ co-activate. It is symmetric ($G_{ij}=G_{ji}$) and positive semidefinite.

**Translation invariance:** a spatial shift permutes the columns $k$ of $F$. Since the sum $\sum_k F_{ik}F_{jk}$ is over *all* $k$, reordering the terms leaves the sum (nearly) unchanged (exactly unchanged for a cyclic/roll permutation, approximately for padded shifts). Hence $G$ barely changes when the image is rolled — style is location-independent.

**Normalization by $C_\ell N_\ell$:** the raw sum grows with the number of channels and spatial positions, so deeper/larger layers would produce larger Gram values and larger gradients. That would make the effective per-layer style weight depend on the layer's resolution. Dividing by $C_\ell N_\ell$ keeps each layer's contribution scale-comparable, so style weights are set by design choice rather than accidentally by layer size.

descriptive.q: Walk through the composite objective $L=\alpha L_{content}+\beta L_{style}+\gamma L_{TV}$. What does each term do, and what happens as you sweep the $\alpha/\beta$ ratio and $\gamma$?
descriptive.answer:
The objective trades off three goals minimized over pixels:
- **$\alpha L_{\text{content}}$** anchors the output to the photo's structure (deep feature maps).
- **$\beta L_{\text{style}}$** pushes the output's Gram statistics toward the painting's, over several layers.
- **$\gamma L_{\text{TV}}$** $= \gamma\sum(|I_{i,j+1}-I_{i,j}|^2+|I_{i+1,j}-I_{i,j}|^2)$ enforces local smoothness.

**Sweeping $\alpha$ with $\beta$ fixed** (e.g., $\alpha\in\{0.1,1,10,100\}$, $\beta=10^6$): low $\alpha$ → "all style, no structure" (content barely visible); high $\alpha$ → the photo with only slight texture.
**Sweeping $\beta$ with $\alpha$ fixed** ($\beta\in\{10^3,\dots,10^6\}$): low $\beta$ → faint stylization; at some large $\beta$ style becomes overpowering and content washes out. It is really the **ratio $\alpha/\beta$** that matters.

**Sweeping $\gamma$:** too low → grainy, high-frequency noise / checkerboard artifacts; too high → over-smoothed, blurry result that loses fine brushstroke texture. Typical guidance: increase $\gamma$ by 10x if noisy, decrease $\beta$ by 10x if content disappears.
